Arnie's Day Trading Setup Playbook

MES · M2K · MNQ · 2-Min Scalping · v5.0 Expanded — LEARN · TRADE · REVIEW
📚 Learn the Theory → Uni's TA Bootcamp 🧮 Position Calculator v2 →
Confirmation-entry win rates · 10 patterns
DB 55–65% · DT 52–62% · Bull Flag 52–60% · Bear Flag 50–58% · 🔨 Hammer/Star 58–67% · 🫂 Engulfing 60–66% · ⭐ Morning/Evening Star 62–68%
All patterns use candle-close confirmation + point-based stops
Double Top — SHORT
⌒⌒
1 · Context Gate ALL required
2 · Pattern Quality Score 0–5
Score
0/5
WAIT
3 · Entry Trigger
Neckline
Lowest close in the valley between peak 1 and peak 2
What this means The "valley" is the pullback dip between the two peaks. The "neckline" is the lowest closing price in that valley — draw a horizontal line there. That line is the floor holding price up. When price breaks below it, the pattern "completes" and the downward move is confirmed. Use closes, not wicks, because closes are where committed positions live.
4 · Stop & Target
Stop
Small point buffer above peak 2 — NOT peak 1 (smaller stop, more size). Buffer by instrument: ES/MES 1.0 pt · NQ 2.0 pt · MNQ 1.5 pt · RTY/M2K 0.4 pt.
What this means A "stop" = a pre-placed order that closes your trade if price moves against you. For a short, place it just above Peak 2 (the most recent high). Why Peak 2 instead of Peak 1? Because if price closes above Peak 2, your whole thesis is wrong — it's making higher highs now, not failing. Putting the stop at the nearer level makes your risk per contract smaller, which means you can trade more contracts for the same dollar risk. Smaller, tighter stops (placed correctly) actually grow your account faster.
Measured Move Target
T1 = Neckline − (Peak − Neckline)
Target = Neckline − (Peak − Neckline)
What this means — "measured move" is a classic projection: Measure the height of the pattern (distance from the peaks down to the neckline). Then subtract that same distance below the neckline — that's where you expect price to go. Example: Peak = 2350, Neckline = 2340. Pattern height = 10 points. Target = 2340 − 10 = 2330. The logic: patterns tend to produce follow-through roughly equal to the pattern that formed them. Not a guarantee, but a reasonable first profit target.
4b · Tight Stop Variant (v5) · Neckline Stop Instead of Peak 2
The classic rule says "stop a small buffer above peak 2." Still valid. But once price has confirmed the breakdown by closing below the neckline, the neckline itself becomes resistance — and a close back above it invalidates the pattern. So an alternative (tighter) stop placement is: a small buffer above the neckline.

Point-based buffers:
MES 1.0 · MNQ 1.5 · M2K 0.4 · ES 1.0 · NQ 2.0 · RTY 0.4 pts above the neckline.
Why it works If price reclaims the neckline as resistance, the thesis is intact even if price bounces toward peak 2. A neckline-break pattern that re-breaks the neckline back up IS a failure — no reason to wait for peak 2. Tighter stop = smaller per-contract risk = same dollar risk with more contracts = bigger win.

Which variant to use: Classic (peak 2) for standalone A-grade patterns. Tight (neckline) for A+/A++ stacked setups where R:R gain is meaningful.
5 · Bust/Failure Flip
Invalidation
Close back above peak 2 after breakdown = pattern busted
What this means "Busted" = the pattern failed and did the opposite of what it was supposed to do. If price broke below the neckline (pattern "worked" briefly) but then reversed and closed back above Peak 2, that's catastrophic failure — sellers got trapped and are now being squeezed. This is a full invalidation, not a "maybe." You're wrong, exit.
6 · Your Two Entry Methods Recommended + Expert Backup

A double top is a short setup, not a long. Buyers tried twice to push price higher and failed both times — sellers are stepping in. You only need two entry methods: the one I recommend for you right now, and the expert backup for when you're more experienced. The "break below" entry is intentionally left out — false breakdowns on 2-min candles are especially vicious on shorts because of the market's long-term upward bias.

Close Below the Neckline — Candle Confirmation Entry Win rate 52–62%
  • What: Wait for the full 2-minute candle to close below the neckline (the middle valley of the M) before entering short.
  • Confirmation that sellers held control for the full 2 minutes.
  • Filters out the bear traps. Higher win rate than entering on the break.
  • Worse entry price — you're shorting lower.
  • Stop (above the M top) is now further away.
  • Note: slightly lower win rate than the double bottom version because of the upward-bias factor — shorts face more reversal risk than longs.
This is the way I recommend for you
Use the candle close below the neckline as your entry for every double top — and be more selective than you are on double bottoms. Markets have a long-term upward bias, so shorting against that bias requires stronger confirmation. Always pair this entry with the selectivity filters in Section 8 below (higher-timeframe check, bearish Stochastic divergence, volume fingerprint). This is the same "wait for the close" rule you use on bear flags, bull flags, and double bottoms — one confirmation habit across every pattern.
Expert Backup — Close Below Neckline, then Pullback Entry Once you're experienced
  • What: Wait for the 2-min candle to close below the neckline (the recommended confirmation). Then don't enter yet. Watch for a small pullback back up to the neckline (now acting as resistance). Enter short on that retest.
  • Confirmation PLUS a better entry price. Tight stop, excellent risk-reward, highest expected value per trade.
  • Same rule you'll use on bear flags — the "close + retest" habit transfers directly.
  • Sometimes the pullback never comes and you miss the trade entirely.
  • Shorting against the market's upward bias is psychologically harder than going long. The discipline bar is higher.
  • Who uses it: experienced professional short-sellers who accept missing trades as part of the game.
Graduation path Trade the recommended method with the selectivity filters for double tops until it's automatic. Only then layer in the Expert Backup — and only on the cleanest setups with strong bearish divergence already in place.
7 · Win Rate Context
Recommended method — candle close below neckline
Roughly 52–62% win rate. With the selectivity filters in Section 8, can improve to 60%+.
For comparison — entering on the break (not recommended)
Roughly 38–48% win rate — slightly worse than bottoms because of the upward-bias factor.
Important caveat for double tops specifically
Markets have a long-term upward bias. Shorting against that bias requires more discipline. Professionals are generally more selective with double top entries than double bottom entries — they wait for stronger confirmation and often require additional context (trend already weakening, lower highs on a higher timeframe, etc.).
8 · Selectivity Filters Mandatory on double tops

The recommended close-below entry alone isn't enough on double tops — you need these three filters every single time. A raw "close below the neckline" double top with no selectivity layer is a losing trade over time.

1 · Higher timeframe check
Check the 5-min or 15-min chart. If that's still in a clear uptrend, skip the trade.
2 · Stochastics 5,3,3 bearish divergence
The right top (Peak 2) should show lower stochastic values than the left top (Peak 1). That's bearish divergence — momentum is fading even though price is matching.
3 · Volume fingerprint
Volume should be lighter on the right top than the left top. Buyers are losing conviction.
4 · Without these filters, win rates drop
With them, win rates can improve to 60%+. The filters are non-negotiable on double tops. A raw close-below entry without the selectivity layer above is a losing trade over time.
Double Bottom — LONG
⌣⌣
1 · Context Gate ALL required
2 · Pattern Quality Score 0–5
Score
0/5
WAIT
3 · Entry Trigger
Neckline
Highest close in the peak between trough 1 and trough 2
What this means Between the two lows, price bounced up before falling back to Trough 2. The highest closing price in that bounce is your neckline — draw a horizontal line there. That line is the ceiling holding price down. When price breaks above it, the pattern "completes" and the upward move is confirmed. Use closes, not wicks, because closes represent where committed money actually sits.
4 · Stop & Target
Stop
Small point buffer below trough 2 — NOT trough 1 (smaller stop, more size). Buffer by instrument: ES/MES 1.0 pt · NQ 2.0 pt · MNQ 1.5 pt · RTY/M2K 0.4 pt.
What this means A "stop" = a pre-placed order that closes your trade if price moves against you. For a long, place it just below Trough 2 (the most recent low). Why Trough 2 instead of Trough 1? Because if price closes below Trough 2, your whole thesis is wrong — it's making lower lows now, not holding support. Placing the stop at the nearer level makes your per-contract risk smaller, which means you can size larger for the same dollar risk. Smaller stops (placed correctly) are how pros grow accounts.
Measured Move Target
T1 = Neckline + (Neckline − Trough)
Target = Neckline + (Neckline − Trough)
What this means — "measured move" is a classic projection: Measure the height of the pattern (distance from the troughs up to the neckline). Then add that same distance above the neckline — that's where you expect price to go. Example: Trough = 2320, Neckline = 2330. Pattern height = 10 points. Target = 2330 + 10 = 2340. The logic: breakouts tend to produce follow-through roughly equal to the pattern that formed them. Not guaranteed, but a reasonable first profit target.
4b · Tight Stop Variant (v5) · Neckline Stop Instead of Trough 2
The classic rule says "stop a small buffer below trough 2." Still valid. But once price has confirmed the breakout by closing above the neckline, the neckline itself becomes support — and a close back below it invalidates the pattern. So an alternative (tighter) stop placement is: a small buffer below the neckline.

Point-based buffers:
MES 1.0 · MNQ 1.5 · M2K 0.4 · ES 1.0 · NQ 2.0 · RTY 0.4 pts below the neckline.
Why it works If price reclaims the neckline as support, the thesis is still intact even if price dips toward trough 2. A neckline-break pattern that re-breaks the neckline in the opposite direction IS a failure of the pattern — no reason to wait for trough 2. Tighter stop = smaller per-contract risk = same dollar risk with more contracts = bigger win when it works.

Tradeoff: tighter stops mean higher chance of being stopped out on noise before the breakout works. Win rate is slightly lower per trade. But R:R improves significantly, and over many trades the math favors the tighter variant on M2K and MES specifically.

Which variant to use: · Classic (trough 2 stop): A-grade patterns where you want maximum "survive the shake" probability and size for the wider stop. · Tight (neckline stop): A+/A++ stacked setups where conviction is high and the R:R gain from a tighter stop is meaningful.
5 · Bust/Failure Flip
Invalidation
Close back below trough 2 after breakout = pattern busted
What this means "Busted" = the pattern failed and reversed. If price broke above the neckline (pattern "worked" briefly) but then reversed and closed back below Trough 2, that's a full failure — buyers got trapped and are now being squeezed. This is invalidation, not a "maybe." You're wrong, exit.
6 · Your Two Entry Methods Recommended + Expert Backup

Every double bottom gives you the same fork in the road. Some traders enter the moment price crosses the neckline; others wait for a full 2-minute candle to close above it. You only need one rule to start with — the one I recommend below — and one expert backup method to graduate into later. The "break above" entry is intentionally left out: it gets trapped too often on M2K and MES 2-minute candles.

Close Above the Neckline — Candle Confirmation Entry Win rate 55–65%
  • What: Wait for the full 2-minute candle to close above the neckline (the middle peak of the W) before entering long.
  • Confirmation. A candle that closes above the neckline tells you buyers held control for the full 2-minute period.
  • Filters out a huge percentage of false breakouts. Win rate goes up meaningfully vs. entering on the break.
  • You give up some of the move — by the time the candle closes, price may already be 1.25–2.00 pts above the neckline (ES/MES/NQ/MNQ) or 0.50–0.80 pts above (RTY/M2K).
  • Risk-reward on each individual trade gets a little worse — entry is higher, stop (below the W bottom) is relatively further away.
  • Who uses it: most professional and institutional short-term traders. They think in terms of expected value, not single-trade outcome. A slightly worse entry that filters out 30–40% of false signals is a massive edge over time.
This is the way I recommend for you
Use the candle close above the neckline as your single entry rule for every double bottom on M2K and MES 2-min charts. Every time you sit through the candle waiting for it to close, you're training yourself to wait for confirmation rather than chase — and that habit pays back for years. This is also the same "wait for the close" rule you use on bull flags, bear flags, and double tops. One consistent confirmation habit across every pattern is far more powerful than switching rules setup-by-setup.
Expert Backup — Close Above Neckline, then Pullback Entry Once you're experienced
  • What: Wait for the 2-min candle to close above the neckline (the recommended confirmation). Then don't enter yet. Watch for a small pullback back to the neckline (now acting as support). Enter long on that retest.
  • Best of both worlds — you get the confirmation AND a better entry price.
  • Stop is tighter, risk-reward is excellent. Highest expected value per trade of any double-bottom method.
  • Requires serious patience — sometimes the pullback never comes and you miss the trade entirely.
  • You'll watch a handful of perfectly valid double bottoms run away without you.
  • Who uses it: experienced professional scalpers who have fully accepted that missing trades is part of the game. Graduate into this once the "close above" method is second nature.
Graduation path Trade the recommended method until it's automatic and you're comfortable with the win rate. Then start running the Expert Backup in parallel on a small number of cleaner setups. When you can sit through the "no pullback, no trade" outcomes without frustration, it becomes your default.
7 · Win Rate Context
Recommended method — candle close above neckline
Roughly 55–65% win rate on intraday timeframes.
For comparison — entering on the break (not recommended)
Roughly 40–50% win rate. That's why we don't use it.
Why the close wins over time Even though the close entry has a slightly worse entry price, the improvement in win rate more than compensates. Over 100 trades, you come out meaningfully ahead. That's the whole game: expected value, not single-trade outcome.
8 · Why This Is the Right Approach for Arnie M2K & MES · 2-min
1 · It forces patience
Every time you sit through that candle waiting for it to close, you're training yourself to wait for confirmation rather than chase. That habit pays back for years.
2 · Algos hunt break-entry traders
On NQ, RTY, M2K and MES specifically, the false-breakout rate on 2-minute candles is brutal. These are fast, algorithmic-driven instruments. Waiting for the close is what keeps you out of those traps.
3 · Expert Backup comes later
Once the close-above method is automatic, graduate into the Expert Backup (close confirmation + pullback entry) for even better risk-reward.
4 · Practical observation tip
When watching a candle form with price above the neckline, notice how often it pulls back below before close versus holds above. That single observation teaches more about market structure than any book.
Bull Flag — LONG (Continuation)
▲▎
1 · What a Bull Flag Is Precision definition

A bull flag is a continuation pattern. That's the critical starting point. You need a prior move up — the flagpole — followed by a brief consolidation that drifts slightly downward or sideways — the flag — before price resumes upward.

The psychology: Aggressive buyers push price up sharply (the pole). Then some buyers take profits and price drifts back. But — and this is the key — sellers can't push it down very far. Supply dries up. Then buyers step back in and the next leg up begins.

On a 2-minute chart in M2K or MES, a complete bull flag typically forms over 4–12 candles (8–24 minutes). If the consolidation drags on much longer than that, it's losing its character as a flag and becoming a range — which is a different trade entirely.

Why "continuation" matters Reversal patterns (double bottom, double top) try to catch a turning point — you're betting the old trend is dying. Continuation patterns (flags) assume the trend is still alive and well — you're just trying to board a moving train during its brief pause. Different logic, different entry rules, different stop placement. Never trade a flag without first confirming the flagpole exists. No pole = no flag = not this pattern.
2 · Anatomy of a Quality Bull Flag
The Flagpole
2–4 strong bullish candles moving up with conviction · M2K: 15–30 points · MES: 8–20 points · Decent bodies (not mostly wick) · Volume above average
What this means The "pole" is the setup move that earns you the right to trade the flag. You want clear, committed buying — full green bodies, limited wicking, rising volume. A pole built mostly of wicks and dojis isn't a real pole; it's indecision dressed up as a move. On a real pole, a chart-glance tells you "buyers are in control right now."
Flag — Retracement depth (HARD RULE)
Must retrace only 25–50% of the flagpole. If pullback exceeds 61.8% (Fib) → pattern invalidated.
What this means Fib = Fibonacci retracement levels (38.2%, 50%, 61.8%) that traders use to measure pullbacks. A shallow pullback (25–50%) means buyers are still in control and just profit-takers are rotating out. A pullback deeper than 61.8% means real selling has appeared, and the pole may be the end of the move rather than the start. This is a hard rule: if price closes through the 61.8% level, stop calling it a bull flag. It's now a deeper correction.
Flag — Volume
Decreasing volume vs. the pole. Heavy volume during pullback = warning sign (real selling, not profit-taking).
What this means On a healthy flag, the pullback bars have visibly smaller volume histograms than the pole bars. Less participation = profit-taking, not genuine selling. If volume stays high or increases during the pullback, it means new sellers are actively hitting bids — that's distribution, not a flag. Pass.
Flag — Slope (CRITICAL)
Slightly downward OR sideways. If it slopes upward → that's an ascending wedge, which is bearish — opposite of what you want.
What this means An ascending wedge looks similar (higher lows, higher highs squeezing together) but behaves opposite to a bull flag. In a wedge, buyers are working harder and harder (higher lows) for smaller and smaller gains (higher highs only slightly higher) — that's exhaustion, and wedges typically break down, not up. If the "flag" is sloping up, you're not looking at a flag anymore. Walk away or flip your thinking.
Flag — Candle size
Candles should be getting smaller (narrower range). Compressing volatility fuels the explosive continuation.
What this means As the flag forms, watch the bars: they should be shrinking — smaller bodies, smaller wicks, tighter range. That compression is a volatility coil. When volatility compresses in a direction-biased market, the expansion out of the coil tends to be sharp. Big bars in the flag = no coiling = weaker breakout.
The Breakout
Price breaks above the upper trendline of the flag channel and resumes the uptrend.
3 · Your Two Entry Methods Recommended + Expert Backup

There are four ways traders enter bull flags. You only need two — the method I recommend for you right now, and the expert backup method you'll graduate into once you have more experience. The other two (break of trendline, break above flagpole high) are intentionally left out because one gets you trapped too often and the other gives you a bad price.

Candle Close Above the Flag Trendline Win rate 52–60%
  • What: Draw a trendline across the highs of the pullback candles. Wait for a full 2-minute candle to close above that trendline — not just poke through — then enter long.
  • Significant filter against false breakouts. Close above = buyers held control for the full 2 minutes.
  • Same confirmation principle as double bottoms — applies even more strongly because flag breakouts fake out more often.
  • Give up some entry price. RTY/M2K: 0.20–0.50 pts worse. ES/MES/NQ/MNQ: 0.25–0.75 pts worse.
  • Occasionally the breakout candle is so large that entry is uncomfortably far from stop.
  • Who uses it: most popular method among competent retail day traders — balances confirmation with reasonable pricing.
This is the way I recommend for you
Use the candle close above the flag trendline as your entry for every bull flag, on every M2K and MES 2-minute chart. It's the single habit that will do the most for your results right now: it removes the guesswork, it makes algorithms hunt someone else, and it matches how you'll trade double bottoms, bear flags, and double tops — one confirmation rule across all four patterns.
Expert Backup — Close Above Trendline, then Pullback Entry Once you're experienced
  • What: Wait for a 2-minute candle to close above the flag trendline (same confirmation as above). Then do not enter yet. Place a limit order at or just above the broken trendline, expecting a brief retest. Enter long when price pulls back to that level.
  • Best of both worlds — confirmation AND a better entry price.
  • Stop is tighter, risk-reward is excellent. Highest expected value per trade of any method.
  • You will miss roughly 30–40% of valid bull flags because price never pulls back — it just goes.
  • Requires serious discipline. You'll watch perfectly good trades leave without you.
  • Who uses it: experienced professional scalpers and prop-desk traders who have fully accepted that missing trades is part of the game. Graduate into this once the "close above" method is second nature.
Graduation path Trade the recommended method until it's automatic and you're comfortable with the win rate. Then start running the Expert Backup in parallel on a small number of setups. When you can sit through the "no pullback, no trade" trades without frustration, it becomes your default.
4 · Stop Loss Placement
Stop A · Below the Flag Low (recommended)
  • What: Place stop just below the lowest point of the flag consolidation (0.25 pt on ES/MES/NQ/MNQ · 0.10 pt on RTY/M2K).
  • Most common placement. Gives trade the most room to work.
  • Typical stop distance: RTY/M2K 1.0–2.0 pts · ES/MES/NQ/MNQ 1.5–3.75 pts.
  • Wider stop but higher probability — not getting shaken out by normal flag volatility.
  • Larger dollar risk per trade. M2K 1.5 pt stop = $7.50/contract. MES 3.0 pt stop = $15/contract. RTY 1.5 pt = $75/contract. ES 3.0 pt = $150/contract.
Stop B · Below the Last Swing Low Within the Flag
  • What: Stop below the most recent swing low inside the flag. Typically 40–60% of Method A's width.
  • Less dollar risk, better risk-reward ratio.
  • Higher chance of getting stopped out on normal flag oscillation before the breakout works.
  • Win rate drops 5–8% vs. Method A.
Stop C · Fixed Dollar or Fixed-Point Stop
  • What: Predetermined max loss (e.g., 0.8 pts on RTY/M2K, 1.5 pts on ES/MES/NQ/MNQ) regardless of chart structure.
  • Consistent risk management, easy position sizing.
  • Stop has no relationship to actual chart structure.
  • Sometimes too tight (stopped out right before the move). Sometimes too wide (risking more than necessary).
What professionals do
Almost universally Stop Method A — let chart structure define the stop, then adjust contract count so dollar risk stays consistent. Wide flag = fewer contracts. Tight flag = more contracts.
4b · Tight Stop Variant (v5) · Trendline-Break Stop
Same principle as the neckline stop on double bottoms. Once price has closed above the flag trendline, that trendline becomes support for the continuation. An alternative (tighter) stop is a small buffer below the broken trendline.

Point-based buffers:
MES 1.0 · MNQ 1.5 · M2K 0.4 · ES 1.0 · NQ 2.0 · RTY 0.4 pts below the broken trendline.
When to use it Use the tight trendline-break stop on A+/A++ stacked setups (e.g., bull flag + morning star at PDL) where conviction is high and you want the R:R gain. Keep Stop A (below flag low) for standalone A-grade patterns where you want maximum room to work.
5 · M2K vs. MES — Specific Differences
M2K (Micro Russell 2000)
Bull flags tend to be choppier and messier than MES. Russell is small-cap — inherently more volatile. Flags often look "dirty" — pullback is jagged, not a clean channel. Need more trendline tolerance. False-breakout rate a few points higher.
· Flagpole moves: 15–30 points on 2-min · Flag retracement: 5–15 points
MES (Micro S&P 500)
Bull flags generally cleaner and more textbook. S&P is large-cap, highly liquid — candle structures smoother. Flags form tighter, more recognizable channels. Pattern reliability slightly higher.
· Flagpole moves: 8–20 points on 2-min · Flag retracement: 3–10 points
Practical implication If learning the pattern, MES gives cleaner examples to practice with. Once consistent, M2K offers more points per move (larger absolute moves) but requires more tolerance for messiness.
6 · What Professionals Actually Do (Full Workflow)

Most professional scalpers on these timeframes use the recommended entry (candle close above the trendline) combined with Stop Method A (below the flag low).

Full professional approach
  • 1. Identify the flag forming in real time as volume decreases and candles compress.
  • 2. Draw the flag trendline.
  • 3. Wait for a 2-minute candle to close above that trendline.
  • 4. Enter at the open of the next candle.
  • 5. Stop goes below the flag low.
  • 6. Target is the flagpole height projected from the breakout point (the measured move).
  • 7. Take partial profits at 1:1 risk-reward. If stop is 1.2 points away, take some off at 1.2 points of profit.
  • 8. Trail the remainder toward the full measured move target.
Time Stop
If the trade hasn't moved meaningfully in your direction within 3–4 candles (6–8 minutes) after entry, exit at scratch or small loss.
Why this rule matters A real bull flag breakout should show follow-through quickly. If it stalls, the pattern has failed even if the stop hasn't been hit. A stalled flag turns into slow-bleed losers. The time stop gets you out before "maybe it'll work" drains your P&L.
7 · Arnie's Specific Approach M2K / 6 contracts / Stoch 5,3,3
1 · Recommended entry — candle close above the flag trendline
Aligns with the same principle on double bottoms. One consistent confirmation habit across multiple patterns > different entry rules per setup.
2 · Stop Method A — below the flag low
Let chart structure define risk. M2K at 6 contracts × $5/pt × 1.5-pt stop = $45 risk. If the flag is sloppy and stop would be 2.5 pts ($75 risk), consider dropping to 4 contracts. (RTY same math at $50/pt: 1.5-pt stop = $450 at 6 contracts — drop contract count for your risk budget.)
3 · Stochastics 5,3,3 confirmation filter
Only take the bull flag breakout if:
· Stochastic is already above 50 and rising, OR
· Stochastic is crossing up from below 20 during the flag formation.
· Warning sign: Stochastic pegged above 80 and rolling over when the flag "breaks out" — momentum may be exhausting.
4 · Measured move target — take half at 1:1
Target = flagpole height projected from breakout point. On M2K: take 3 contracts off at +1.2 pts, let the other 3 ride toward the full target with a trailing stop.
5 · Time stop — exit if no movement in 3 candles
If trade hasn't moved at least 1.25–1.50 pts (ES/MES/NQ/MNQ) or 0.50–0.60 pts (RTY/M2K) in your direction within 3 candles (6 minutes), get out. This single rule will save you from a huge number of grinding, slow-bleed losers.
6 · During practice phase — screenshot every flag
Wins and losses. After a week, review them all at once. Patterns emerge in which flags worked and which didn't that no amount of theory can teach. Visual pattern recognition only develops through repetition and review.
Bear Flag — SHORT (Continuation)
▼▎
1 · What a Bear Flag Is Mirror of bull flag

A bear flag is the mirror image of a bull flag — a continuation pattern for downtrends. You need a prior move down — the flagpole — followed by a brief consolidation that drifts slightly upward or sideways — the flag — before price resumes downward.

The psychology: Aggressive sellers push price down sharply (the pole). Then some sellers cover their shorts and price drifts back up. But buyers can't push it up very far. Demand is weak. Then sellers step back in and the next leg down begins.

On a 2-minute chart in M2K or MES, a complete bear flag typically forms over 4–12 candles (8–24 minutes). If consolidation drags on much longer, it's losing its character as a flag and becoming a range.

2 · Critical Difference · Bear Flags Are Faster & More Violent
Why bear flags resolve faster than bull flags
1. Fear moves markets faster than greed.
2. Short covering during the flag creates artificial buying that vanishes the moment the breakdown begins.
3. Stop losses below the flag trigger cascading sell orders.
Practical implication Entries on bear flags need to happen more quickly, and targets are often hit faster than expected. Don't be greedy — take profits aggressively. This is the single biggest behavioral difference between trading the two flag directions.
3 · Anatomy of a Quality Bear Flag
The Flagpole (Down Move)
2–4 strong bearish candles moving down with conviction · M2K: 15–30 point drop · MES: 8–20 points · Decent-sized red bodies · Volume ideally above average
Flag — Retracement depth (HARD RULE)
Must retrace only 25–50% of the flagpole. If bounce exceeds 61.8% (Fib) → pattern invalidated. It's now a deeper bounce that may signal reversal, not a bear flag.
Flag — Volume
Decreasing volume vs. the pole. Heavy volume during the bounce = warning sign that sellers may be losing control (real buying is appearing).
Flag — Slope (CRITICAL)
Slightly upward OR sideways. If it slopes downward → that's a descending wedge, which is bullish — opposite of what you want for a short.
Flag — Candle size
Candles should be getting smaller (narrower range). Compressing volatility fuels the explosive continuation down.
The Breakdown
Price breaks below the lower trendline of the flag channel and resumes the downtrend.
4 · Your Two Entry Methods Recommended + Expert Backup

Bear flags have four classic entry methods too — but you only need two. The method I recommend for you right now, and the expert backup you'll graduate into later. Bear flags move faster than bull flags, so getting the confirmation right matters even more.

Candle Close Below the Flag Trendline Win rate 50–58%
  • What: Draw a trendline across the lows of the bounce candles inside the flag. Wait for a full 2-minute candle to close below that trendline — not just poke through on a wick — then enter short.
  • Confirmation that sellers held control for the full 2 minutes.
  • Filters out a large portion of bear traps. Significantly higher win rate than entering on the break.
  • Worse entry price (lower). RTY/M2K: 0.20–0.50 pts worse · ES/MES/NQ/MNQ: 0.25–0.75 pts worse.
  • Bear flags move fast — sometimes a chunk of the move is already gone by the close.
  • Note: slightly lower win rate than the bull-flag version because of the speed factor — by the time you confirm, some moves are exhausted.
This is the way I recommend for you
Use the candle close below the flag trendline as your entry on every bear flag. Same confirmation habit you use on bull flags and double bottoms — one rule across every pattern. Bear flags punish impatience more than any other pattern on this list; the close filter is what protects you from the bear traps that snap violently back up into the flag.
Expert Backup — Close Below Trendline, then Pullback Entry Once you're experienced
  • What: Wait for a 2-min candle to close below the trendline (same confirmation as above). Then don't enter yet. Place a limit order at or just below the broken trendline, expecting a brief bounce back up to it. Enter short when price retests that level (now acting as resistance).
  • Confirmation plus a better entry price. Tight stop, excellent risk-reward, highest expected value per trade.
  • Bear flags often don't pull back to retest — they move too fast.
  • You will miss approximately 40–50% of valid bear flag trades (higher miss rate than bull flags due to speed).
  • Hardest method psychologically. Watching a valid short run away without you is genuinely painful.
  • Who uses it: experienced scalpers only. Graduate into this after the "close below" method is automatic.
Graduation path Trade the recommended method exclusively on bear flags until the rule is second nature. Once you can reliably take the close-below entry without hesitation, layer the Expert Backup in as a secondary setup for the cleanest, slowest bear flags only.
5 · Stop Loss Placement
Stop A · Above the Flag High (recommended)
  • What: Place stop just above the highest point of the flag consolidation (0.25 pt on ES/MES/NQ/MNQ · 0.10 pt on RTY/M2K).
  • Most common. Gives trade most room to work.
  • Typical stop distance: RTY/M2K 1.0–2.0 pts · ES/MES/NQ/MNQ 1.5–3.75 pts.
Stop B · Above the Last Swing High Within the Flag
  • What: Tighter stop, 40–60% of Method A width.
  • Less dollar risk.
  • Higher chance of getting stopped out on normal flag oscillation. Win rate drops 5–8% vs. Method A.
Stop C · Fixed Dollar or Fixed-Point Stop
  • What: Predetermined max loss regardless of structure.
  • Easy position sizing.
  • No relationship to actual chart structure.
What professionals do
Stop Method A — chart-structure-based — and adjust contract count to keep dollar risk consistent.
4b · Tight Stop Variant (v5) · Trendline-Break Stop
Mirror of bull flag. Once price has closed below the flag trendline, that trendline becomes resistance for the continuation down. An alternative (tighter) stop is a small buffer above the broken trendline.

Point-based buffers:
MES 1.0 · MNQ 1.5 · M2K 0.4 · ES 1.0 · NQ 2.0 · RTY 0.4 pts above the broken trendline.
When to use it Use the tight trendline-break stop on A+/A++ stacked setups (e.g., bear flag + evening star at PDH) where conviction is high. Keep Stop A (above flag high) for standalone A-grade patterns. Because bear flags move violently, the tight variant gets stopped less often than the symmetric bull-flag case — sellers rarely come back to retest the broken trendline from below.
6 · M2K vs. MES — Bear Flag Differences
M2K (Micro Russell 2000)
Bear flags more violent than MES — small caps drop harder than large caps. More choppy and "dirty" patterns. Need more tolerance in trendline placement.
· Flagpole drops: 15–30 points on 2-min · Flag bounce: 5–15 points
· More profit potential per trade but more whipsaw risk.
MES (Micro S&P 500)
Cleaner bear flag patterns. More predictable behavior.
· Flagpole drops: 8–20 points · Flag bounce: 3–10 points
· More reliable but smaller absolute moves.
7 · Trading Against the Long-Term Trend (CRITICAL)

Both M2K and MES have long-term upward bias. Trading bear flags means trading against that bias on a short timeframe. This requires:

Extra discipline required for bear flags
  • More selectivity than bull flags.
  • Faster execution of profit-taking — don't get greedy.
  • Awareness of broader context — bear flags during a strong uptrend day fail more often than bear flags during a clearly down day.
  • Higher-timeframe check — if 5-min or 15-min chart is in clear uptrend, lower the priority of bear flag setups.
8 · Professional Bear Flag Workflow

Most professional scalpers use the recommended entry (candle close below trendline) with Stop Method A (above flag high).

Full professional approach
  • 1. Identify the flag forming as volume decreases and candles compress.
  • 2. Draw the flag trendline (across the lows of the bounce).
  • 3. Check higher-timeframe context — is broader trend supportive of a short?
  • 4. Wait for a 2-minute candle to close below the trendline.
  • 5. Enter short at the open of the next candle.
  • 6. Stop above the flag high.
  • 7. Target is flagpole height projected from the breakdown point (measured move).
  • 8. Take profits more aggressively than on bull flags — half off at 1:1, trail the rest with a tight stop.
  • 9. Time stop: if not moving within 2–3 candles (4–6 minutes), exit. Bear flags move fast — if it's not moving, the pattern has failed.
9 · Arnie's Specific Approach M2K / 6 contracts / Stoch 5,3,3
1 · Recommended entry — candle close below the flag trendline
Same confirmation principle as all other patterns. Consistency across patterns builds discipline.
2 · Stop Method A — above the flag high
Let chart structure define risk. M2K at 6 contracts × $5/pt × 1.5-pt stop = $45 risk. If stop would be 2.5 pts ($75), drop to 4 contracts. (RTY at $50/pt: 1.5-pt stop = $450 at 6 contracts — scale contract count to fit your risk budget.)
3 · Stochastics 5,3,3 confirmation filter
Only take the bear flag breakdown if:
· Stochastic is already below 50 and falling, OR
· Stochastic is crossing down from above 80 during the flag formation.
· Warning sign: Stochastic pegged below 20 and turning up when the flag "breaks down" — downside momentum may be exhausting.
4 · Higher timeframe filter (CRITICAL for bear flags)
Before taking any bear flag, glance at the 15-minute chart:
· 15-min clear uptrend → skip the trade or reduce to 2–3 contracts
· 15-min sideways or down → take normal position size
This single filter dramatically improves bear flag win rate.
5 · Measured move target — take profits faster
Take half off at 1:1 (faster than bull flag because bear flags reverse violently). Trail the remainder with a tighter stop than on a bull flag.
6 · Tighter time stop than bull flags
If trade hasn't moved at least 1.25–1.50 pts (ES/MES/NQ/MNQ) or 0.50–0.60 pts (RTY/M2K) in your direction within 2 candles (4 minutes) of entry, exit. Bear flags either work fast or they don't work — don't sit in a stalled bear flag.
7 · During practice phase — screenshot every bear flag
Wins AND losses. Pay special attention to the higher-timeframe context on losers — most losing bear flags occurred against a clear higher-timeframe uptrend.
🔨 Hammer & Shooting Star — Single-Candle Reversals
🔨 / ⭐
1-candle signals · MES & M2K & MNQ 2-min · 58–67% win rate with confirmation stack
The fastest reversal signals in the playbook. One candle tells you buyers (hammer) or sellers (shooting star) just crushed the opposing side. No waiting for a double bottom to form — when the rejection is already on the screen, the only thing left is confirmation. This tab goes deep: structure, psychology, Bulkowski stats, context, five-point quality score, entry trigger, point-based stops, tight-stop variant, three-level scale-out, bust rules, kill switches, and the A++ pattern stack.
1 · What a Hammer / Shooting Star Is Single-candle rejection

A hammer (also called a bullish pin bar) is a single candle that appears at the bottom of a downtrend and tells you buyers just fought back from much lower prices. A shooting star is the exact mirror at the top of an uptrend. They're the same underlying signal — a rejection of an extreme — just at opposite ends of the market.

Structure of a hammer
· Small body in the top third of the candle's range
· Long lower wick at least 2× the length of the body
· Little or no upper wick
· Color of the body mostly doesn't matter (slight preference for green, but red hammers work)
Structure of a shooting star
Exact vertical flip: small body in the bottom third, long upper wick ≥2× body, little or no lower wick.
The psychology
During that candle, price was driven hard in one direction — then the opposite side slammed it back before the close. You are literally watching a battle end in real time. The long wick is the evidence of the losing side; the small body near the winning side's end of the range is the evidence of the winning side. When you see a textbook hammer at support, buyers didn't "might" step in — they already did, and you have proof on the screen.
Reliability caveat (from Bulkowski) Bulkowski's raw statistics show hammer at 60.3% reversal rate and shooting star at 59%. He calls these "near random" in isolation. That's true — in isolation. With the confirmation candle rule we use (next candle closes above the hammer's high / below the star's low), plus support/resistance context, plus volume, the practical win rate jumps into the 60–67% range for 2-min scalps. The edge lives in the combination, not the single candle.
2 · Context Gate ALL required — no exceptions
3 · Setup Quality Score 0–5 · score before you enter
Score
0/5
WAIT
How to use the score
4–5 = A-grade · size up.  ·  3 = B-grade · reduced size.  ·  0–2 = skip.
What this means The score is not decoration — it's the single filter between you and a losing trade. Every hammer on the screen looks tradeable. Only the ones scoring 4–5 actually are. Being honest with yourself at the score stage is the difference between 58% win rate and 67% win rate.
4 · Entry Trigger Recommended + Expert Backup
Recommended entry
Wait for the next 2-min candle to close above the hammer's high (for long) or below the shooting star's low (for short), then enter at the open of the bar after that confirmation close.
Why the close, not the touch
  • The close is committed money. Any candle can poke above a hammer's high intrabar and then collapse — only the close confirms buyers held the gain for the full 2 minutes.
  • Why above the high, not just above the close: the high of the hammer is where the buyers finished pushing. A candle closing above that line is a candle that broke through the entire resistance of the rejection.
Expert Backup — Limit Pullback Entry Once you're experienced
  • What: After the confirmation candle closes, place a limit order to enter on a pullback to the hammer's close or even its midpoint.
  • Better price, tighter stop.
  • Frequently missed — hammers often run without pulling back.
  • Graduate into this only after the recommended method is automatic.
5 · Stop & Target Point-based buffer · R:R ≥ 2:1

Stop goes just below the hammer's wick tip (for longs) or just above the shooting star's wick tip (for shorts). The wick tip IS the invalidation — if price trades back down to and through the wick tip, the rejection failed and there's no thesis left.

Buffer below the wick tip — by instrument (all values in points)
· MES: 1.0 pt below the wick tip
· MNQ: 1.5 pt below (noisier)
· M2K: 0.4 pt below (calmer)
· ES: 1.0 pt below
· NQ: 2.0 pt below (noisiest)
· RTY: 0.4 pt below
What this means The old conventional wisdom used fixed-unit buffers that measured differently across contracts. Throw that away. On MES, 0.75 points is too tight for normal 2-min wick-noise on a volatile instrument. On M2K, 0.30 points is also too tight. The point-based buffers above are tuned to each instrument's actual noise floor. A properly placed stop gets hit only when the pattern actually failed, not because of a small wick poke.
⚠ Critical pre-trade filter — the wick-length test
Before taking any hammer/star, calculate: (distance from entry to nearest logical target) ÷ (distance from entry to stop). If that ratio < 2, skip the trade.
Why this matters A textbook hammer with a 12-point wick into a 5-point-away resistance is a bad trade even though the pattern is pretty. The wick length is the risk. Do the math before you click. This is the single filter that keeps beautiful losing trades off your account.
Tight-Stop Variant For A-grade hammers with 4× wick
  • What: Instead of placing the stop below the wick tip, place it at the hammer's body midpoint + the instrument's buffer.
  • Why: When the wick is 4×+ the body length, the whole wick distance is insurance you don't need — if price even returns to the body, the rejection is already failing. A midpoint-body stop cuts risk roughly in half, doubling effective R:R.
  • Trade-off: Higher stop-out rate (~15–20% more) because normal 2-min noise can clip the midpoint on a healthy trade.
  • When to use: ONLY with A-grade setups (score 4–5), wick ≥ 4× body, and an obvious momentum follow-through expected. Never on B-grade setups.
6 · Target Management Scale out in thirds · BE after T1
T1 · 50% scale at 1.5R
Move stop to break-even after T1. You are now risk-free on the remaining runners. This is the single most important exit rule in the playbook.
What this means "1.5R" means 1.5 times your original stop distance in your favor. If your stop was 2 points away, T1 is 3 points above entry. Scaling 50% off here covers a portion of your theoretical max profit early and locks in emotional certainty that this trade is no longer a loser. Moving the stop to break-even then removes downside entirely — the remaining 50% rides for free.
T2 · 25% scale at 2.5R (or next visible level)
Whichever comes first. "Next visible level" = PDH/PDL, VWAP on the opposite side, prior swing, round number. If a known level sits between 1.5R and 2.5R, take T2 there — never let a good target pass expecting a better one.
T3 · 25% runner
Trail behind 2-min swing points. Exit on close through the trailing stop. This is the ticket that occasionally doubles the trade's profit when follow-through is unusually strong.
⏱ Time stop · 3 candles (6 min)
If price hasn't moved ≥1R in your favor within 3 candles of entry, exit at scratch. A real hammer/star breakout should show follow-through quickly — if it's still hanging around entry after 6 minutes, the bounce wasn't real.
Why time matters on single-candle patterns Hammers/stars are supposed to produce immediate follow-through because they are pure momentum signals. If six minutes pass and the move hasn't materialized, the market has already told you the pattern didn't take. Exiting at scratch is free — eating a full stop-out when momentum died is not.
7 · Bust / Failure Invalidation and the flip option

Invalidation = close back past the wick tip within 2 candles of entry. Bust follow-through is meaningful — consider a flip (short after failed hammer, long after failed shooting star) if there's a follow-up candle closing beyond the wick in the opposite direction, AND higher-timeframe context supports it.

The psychology of a flipped hammer
When a hammer forms, every observer assumes buyers just won. Stops collect below the wick tip. Longs pile in on the confirmation. When price then closes back below the wick, those longs are trapped — their stops fire, momentum accelerates downward, and you get a clean trend continuation. This is why failed reversals often produce the cleanest continuations.
How to trade the flip Wait for the close back past the wick. Enter short at the open of the next bar. Stop at the high of the failed hammer. Target the prior swing low, or 2R — whichever comes first. Size reduced relative to the original setup — flips are secondary confirmations, not A-grade primaries.
8 · Kill Switches Don't take the trade
Skip the trade if ANY of these are true
  • Wick is less than 2× the body → not a real hammer/star, skip
  • No prior trend (≥2 legs in the opposite direction) → nothing to reverse, skip
  • Random price level (no PDH/PDL/ORH/ORL/VWAP/round # within 1R) → no institutional interest, skip
  • News candle — CPI, FOMC, NFP, PCE, PPI within 30 minutes → random volatility, skip
  • R:R below 2:1 after the point-based stop placement → the math doesn't work, skip
  • Wick length below instrument minimum (MES 2 · MNQ 3 · M2K 1.0 · ES 2 · NQ 4 · RTY 1.0 pts) → noise, not signal, skip
  • Volume on the hammer/star bar is below recent average → no conviction, skip
  • Already hit daily limit (2 losses or 3 trades) → stop trading, not negotiable
9 · Win Rate Context Know where the edge lives
Break-entry (no confirmation)
~50–55% — near random per Bulkowski
Confirmation-close entry
~58–62% — the recommended method
Confirmation + level + divergence (A-grade)
~62–67% — stacked edge
10 · Arnie's Specific Approach Confirmation · Point-based stop · Wick test · Calculator · Scale · Stack
1 · Entry
Confirmation candle close above hammer high / below star low. Enter at open of next bar.
2 · Stop
Point-based buffer beyond wick tip (see table above). Everything is in points.
3 · Wick test before entry
Target distance must be ≥ 2× stop distance, else skip.
4 · Sizing
Use the Position Calculator v2 reverse mode: target $200 risk, stop distance known, calc gives contracts.
5 · Scale
50% at 1.5R + BE stop, 25% at T2, 25% runner trailing 2-min swings.
6 · Stack for A++
A hammer at the low of a forming double bottom at PDL is elite. See the 🔗 Pattern Stacking tab.
🫂 Bullish & Bearish Engulfing — Two-Candle Reversals
▼▲
2-candle signals · committed opposite-side takeover · 60–66% win rate with confirmation
One of Bulkowski's highest-ranked candle patterns. When a green candle's body fully swallows the prior red candle's body (bullish) or vice versa (bearish), you're watching one side overwhelm the other in a single 2-minute window. It's louder than a hammer and earlier than a three-candle morning star — the sweet spot.
1 · What an Engulfing Is Two candles · committed takeover
Bullish engulfing structure
· Candle 1: red (any size — larger is cleaner)
· Candle 2: green, and its body (open-to-close) completely swallows candle 1's body from below open to above close.
· Wicks don't count for engulfing — only bodies.
Bearish engulfing structure
Exact mirror: green candle 1, then red candle 2 whose body engulfs candle 1's body.
Why body, not wicks
Bodies represent committed money — they're where most of the session's trading happened and where positions got accumulated. Wicks are test-and-rejected prices. An engulfing signal that's built on bodies tells you the new side is actually trading at sustained prices beyond where the old side could hold.
The psychology of takeover On candle 1, sellers pushed price to a new low with full conviction — the body is the evidence of how much they committed. On candle 2, buyers showed up and didn't just fight back — they pushed price all the way through the committed selling range and closed above where candle 1 began. That's not a defense, that's a rout. Every seller who opened a position during candle 1 is now trapped underwater by the close of candle 2. They'll have to cover, and that covering fuels follow-through.
Bulkowski's reliability data
Bullish engulfing: 63% reversal rate (rank 22 of 103 candlestick patterns — respectable). Bearish engulfing: slightly lower raw rank because markets have long-term upward bias. With confirmation close, support/resistance context, and volume expansion, practical 2-min scalping win rates land at ~62–66% bullish, ~58–62% bearish.
Why engulfing beats hammer Hammers rely on one bar's worth of evidence. Engulfings rely on two bars of evidence AND require the second to override the first in both direction and magnitude. That extra requirement is a harder bar to clear — which is exactly why fewer pretty patterns make it through, and the ones that do have a higher conversion rate.
2 · Context Gate ALL required
3 · Setup Quality Score 0–5 · score before you enter
Score
0/5
WAIT
How to use the score
4–5 = A-grade · size up.  ·  3 = B-grade · reduced size.  ·  0–2 = skip.
4 · Entry Trigger Faster than hammer — enter on the close
Recommended entry
At the close of the engulfing candle (not the next bar). Engulfing signals are complete at their own close — waiting for the next bar often gives up a meaningful chunk of the initial move.
Expert Backup — Market-on-open of next bar For bracket-order traders
  • What: Enter at the open of the next bar at market.
  • Same timing, slightly different execution style for traders with pre-programmed bracket orders.
  • Tiny slippage vs. close entry — usually 0.25 points on MES.
5 · Stop & Target Point-based buffer · R:R ≥ 2:1

Stop goes just below the engulfing candle's low (bullish) or just above its high (bearish) — using the extreme of candle 2's wick, not candle 1's.

Point-based buffers (same as hammer)
· MES 1.0 · MNQ 1.5 · M2K 0.4 · ES 1.0 · NQ 2.0 · RTY 0.4 pts beyond the engulfing bar's extreme.
Why candle 2's extreme, not candle 1's Candle 2 is the "new" information. If price closes back below candle 2's low, the takeover reversed — the thesis is dead. Candle 1's low is already far enough away to make the R:R unfavorable, and it's no longer the active invalidation point.
Tight-Stop Variant For A-grade engulfings with ≥150% body
  • What: Place stop at the midpoint of the engulfing candle's body + instrument buffer, instead of at its low.
  • Why: For a 150%+ engulfing bar, a close back below the midpoint already means the takeover failed. The full-bar-low stop is insurance you don't need.
  • Trade-off: Stop-out rate rises ~15% because normal post-engulfing retracement can clip the midpoint.
  • When to use: Only with score 4–5 AND body ≥ 150% AND clear next-level target that allows 3:1+ R:R with the tight stop.
⚠ R:R sanity check
(Target distance) ÷ (stop distance) must be ≥ 2. If it's 1.8 or 1.9, skip. A prettier setup will come.
6 · Target Management Scale out in thirds · BE after T1
T1 · 50% scale at 1.5R · move stop to BE
Same rule, same discipline. This is the exit that de-risks the trade and lets the remaining half ride without stress.
T2 · 25% scale at 2.5R or next visible level
Whichever comes first. Engulfings often cross named levels cleanly — if T2 would land just past PDH, take it at PDH instead.
T3 · 25% runner
Trail 2-min swings. Exit on close through trailing stop.
⏱ Time stop · 3 candles (6 min)
Engulfings are fast signals — if there's no ≥1R progress in 6 minutes, the takeover didn't stick. Scratch.
7 · Bust / Failure Failed engulfings often flip cleanly

Close back past the engulfing bar's extreme (low for bullish, high for bearish) = bust. Consider flip if higher-timeframe supports it.

Why failed engulfings flip cleanly
The engulfing bar is a magnet for aggressive longs. When price closes back below it, every long who bought in on the signal is instantly offside. Their stops cluster just below the engulfing bar's low. One more bar closing below that level = stop cascade = easy continuation short. Among failed reversal patterns, engulfing busts have the highest flip conversion rate (~65%).
8 · Kill Switches Skip the trade
Skip if ANY of these are true
  • Engulfing body < 120% of prior body → not a real engulfing, skip
  • Bodies span < 50% of the average recent range → too small to matter, skip
  • No prior trend of ≥2 legs → nothing to reverse, skip
  • Random location (no PDH/PDL/ORH/ORL/VWAP/round # within 1R) → no story, skip
  • News window (30 min around CPI/FOMC/NFP/PCE/PPI) → random volatility, skip
  • R:R < 2:1 after point-based stop placement → math doesn't work, skip
  • Volume on the engulfing bar is below recent average → body without conviction, skip
  • Already hit daily limit → stop trading, not negotiable
9 · Win Rate Context Know where the edge lives
Break entry (no confirmation)
50–55% — near coin-flip
Engulfing-close entry
60–65% — the recommended method
+ level + volume + divergence (A-grade)
64–68% — stacked edge
+ stacked with another pattern (DB/DT/flag)
70–75% — pattern stack (see 🔗 Pattern Stacking tab)
10 · Arnie's Specific Approach Close-of-engulfing · Point stop · Body test · Calculator · Scale · Stack
1 · Entry
At the close of the engulfing candle (not next bar).
2 · Stop
Point-based buffer beyond engulfing bar extreme.
3 · Body size test
Must be ≥120% of prior body, ideally 150%+.
4 · Sizing
Via calculator reverse mode.
5 · Scale
50% / 25% / 25% at 1.5R / 2.5R / runner.
6 · Stack
Bullish engulfing right at a double bottom's neckline break = A++.
⭐ Morning & Evening Star — Three-Candle Reversals
▼ · ▫ · ▲
3-candle signals · slowest but strongest single-pattern reversal · 62–68% win rate
A morning star takes three candles — the long red, the small-body star, then the explosive green — to tell its story. That slower build is a feature, not a bug: by candle 3, you have layers of evidence instead of a single bar's worth. Bulkowski ranked it among the most reliable bullish reversals in his encyclopedia.
1 · What a Morning / Evening Star Is Three acts · one reversal
Morning star · three-candle formation
· Candle 1: a long red candle (strong selling)
· Candle 2: a small-body candle (either color, sometimes a doji if open = close). Represents indecision — the downtrend has stalled.
· Candle 3: a strong green candle that closes above the midpoint of candle 1's body (ideally above its open).
Evening star · mirror
Long green, small star, strong red closing below the midpoint of the first green.
The psychology in three acts
· Candle 1: sellers in full control, no hesitation. They print a long red bar and look strong.
· Candle 2: sellers exhausted. Nobody's willing to push further. The body is small because buyers and sellers fought to a draw. That pause is the first tell that the downtrend is dying.
· Candle 3: buyers step in with conviction — not just defending the prior low, but reclaiming more than half of the last red candle's range. Every seller who opened during candle 1 is now pinned underwater.
Why three candles beats one The beauty of the pattern is that each candle adds a new layer of evidence. By the close of candle 3 you've seen: exhaustion → hesitation → commitment. That's why morning star beats hammer in reliability despite being slower. The trade-off: you give up the first 1.5–2 minutes of the move to earn the extra confidence. Most traders get that trade-off wrong and chase hammers they should have skipped.
Bulkowski reliability
Bulkowski's data places the morning star at 60–65% bullish reversal rate on raw stats, climbing to ~72% when combined with support-level context and other indicators. On a 2-min chart with scalping timeframes this practical accuracy lands near 62–68% with confirmation, 66–72% stacked with divergence and named level.
2 · Context Gate Requires 3-leg prior trend · ALL required
3 · Setup Quality Score 0–5 · score before you enter
Score
0/5
WAIT
How to use the score
5 = A-grade · size up confidently.  ·  4 = still strong, take with full size.  ·  3 = reduced size only.  ·  0–2 = skip.
Why morning stars have a higher score bar Because the pattern requires three candles to form, you're committing 6 minutes of chart time to the setup before entering. That's a longer window for something to go wrong. Demand higher quality before clicking.
4 · Entry Trigger At the close of candle 3 — pattern is complete
Recommended entry
At the close of candle 3. The pattern is complete at that moment — don't wait for a fourth-bar confirmation.
Expert Backup — Pullback to candle 2's high Better price
  • What: Enter on a small retracement back to candle 2's high, for a better price and tighter stop.
  • Better fill, better R:R.
  • Often missed entirely after a strong candle 3 closes — the market rarely pulls back that deep.
  • Graduate into this only after the recommended method is automatic.
5 · Stop & Target Below candle 2's low · point-based

Stop goes just below candle 2's low (the "star"). Candle 2 is the actual bottom of the pattern, and a close back below it means the pattern failed — the "exhaustion" was just a pause, not a turn.

Point-based buffers (same table across all patterns)
· MES 1.0 · MNQ 1.5 · M2K 0.4 · ES 1.0 · NQ 2.0 · RTY 0.4 pts beyond candle 2's extreme.
Why C2's low, not C1's low Candle 2 is the lowest point of the formed pattern. If price closes below C2, the pattern structure is broken — no more "three-act sequence." Candle 1's low is farther down and using it as the stop unnecessarily doubles risk. The point of a morning star stop is: "invalidate at the structural bottom of the pattern, not the bottom of the prior trend."
Tight-Stop Variant For score=5 A-grade morning stars
  • What: Place stop at the midpoint between C2's low and C1's midpoint + instrument buffer.
  • Why: For a textbook A-grade setup where C3 closed above C1's open, price re-touching C2's low is already a serious warning sign. Waiting for the full stop gives up too much R:R.
  • Trade-off: ~15–20% higher stop-out rate.
  • When: Only score=5 morning stars with obvious T2 level within 3R, making the tighter stop produce clean 3:1 R:R.
6 · Target Management Larger follow-through — give the runner room
T1 · 50% scale at 1.5R · move stop to BE
Same de-risk rule as all patterns. Morning stars often hit T1 within 2 bars of entry because the pattern releases a lot of pent-up energy.
T2 · 25% scale at 2.5R or next visible level
Morning stars frequently reach VWAP or an overhead resistance level quickly — if that level lies between 1.5R and 2.5R, take T2 there instead.
T3 · 25% runner — let it run to 4R+
This is where morning stars pay for the slower formation time. After an extended 3-leg down-move, the reversal often carries for 15–20 bars. Trail wider stops on the runner (prior 4-bar swing lows instead of immediate swings) to let the move develop.
⏱ Time stop · 5 candles (10 min)
Morning stars get slightly more time than hammer/engulfing (5 candles instead of 3) because the setup is already slower and follow-through can take longer to develop. If no ≥1R progress in 10 minutes, exit at scratch.
7 · Bust / Failure Failed morning stars often continue the downtrend

Close below candle 2's low = bust. Failed morning stars often lead to continuation of the original downtrend — the pause was a trap, and the return to downside pressure tends to be fast because all the bottom-pickers are now trapped long.

Why failed morning stars produce clean flips
The morning star is a magnet for bottom-pickers. When it fails after a 3-leg downtrend, those bottom-pickers unload their longs the moment C2's low breaks — their stops sit right there. That sell pressure stacks with the continuation of the original trend, often producing 3–4R down-moves within 10–15 bars. Flip-shorts after failed morning stars are among the highest-win-rate short setups in the book.
8 · Kill Switches Skip the trade
Skip if ANY of these are true
  • Candle 3 doesn't close above candle 1's midpoint → not a valid morning star, skip
  • Candle 2 is too big (>30% of candle 1's range) → no "exhaustion pause" evidence, skip
  • Candle 1 is too small (body <60% of recent average) → no exhaustion case, skip
  • No prior 3-leg trend → not enough exhaustion, skip
  • Random location (no named level within 1R) → no story, skip
  • News window (30 min around CPI/FOMC/NFP/PCE/PPI) → random 3-bar sequence, skip
  • R:R below 2:1 after point-based stop placement → math doesn't work, skip
  • Volume on C3 is below volume of C1 → weak handoff, skip
  • Already hit daily limit → stop trading, not negotiable
9 · Win Rate Context Know where the edge lives
Break / anticipation entry (before C3 closes)
55–60% — premature, don't do this
Close-of-candle-3 entry
62–68% — recommended method
+ named level + volume match
66–72% — quality-gated setup
+ stacked with divergence (A-grade)
70–75% — elite setup (see 🔗 Pattern Stacking tab)
10 · Arnie's Specific Approach Close-of-C3 · Point stop · Midpoint test · Calculator · Scale · Stack
1 · Entry
At close of candle 3.
2 · Stop
Point-based buffer below candle 2's low.
3 · Mandatory midpoint test
Candle 3 MUST close above candle 1's midpoint. No midpoint reclaim = no trade.
4 · Sizing
Via calculator.
5 · Scale + BE after T1
Standard 50/25/25 with runner getting extra room.
6 · Stack for elite A++
Morning star with bullish divergence on stochastics at PDL/S-zone = one of the highest-edge setups in the book.
🎯 Failed Breakout Reversal · Highest-Edge Trap Setup
⇄
★ Arnie's A+ Setup
This is the highest-edge setup in the whole playbook. Win rate 60–65% at a clean 2:1 R:R on MES & M2K 2-min. Why? You're not predicting the breakout — you're waiting for it to fail, then fading the trapped crowd as they puke out. Breakouts fail more often than most traders realize, especially at significant levels. Let them try, let them fail, then take their money.
1 · What Is a Failed Breakout? (and why it has edge)
Definition
  • Price breaks above (or below) a well-defined level.
  • Within 2–4 candles, it trades back through the broken level.
  • A "reclaim" candle closes back inside the prior range.
What this means in plain English A "breakout" happens every time price pushes through a line everyone's watching (a prior high, a range edge, a round number). When it works, late buyers pile in and price runs. When it fails, those late buyers are suddenly trapped — they bought high, and now price is falling back under the line they just bought above. Their stops are packed just below the breakout level. When price gets back there and starts triggering those stops, the exits cascade and the move accelerates in the OPPOSITE direction. You're not trading the breakout — you're trading the cascading exits of the people who traded the breakout. That's where the 60–65% win rate comes from.
Why it has edge — the three-part reason
  • Asymmetric pain: breakout traders bought the top of the move; they're underwater the moment it fails.
  • Stops are stacked: a predictable cluster of stop-losses just beyond the level acts as fuel for your trade.
  • You're not guessing — you're reacting: the breakout already happened and already failed. You have evidence before you risk money.
2 · Where It Works Best — Five Significant Levels
Why these five levels (and not random swing points)
  • Previous Day High / Low (PDH / PDL): every institutional desk has these marked. Biggest, most watched level in intraday trading.
  • Opening Range (ORB): the high and low of the first 15 minutes. Day-trading community obsesses over this; the algos run right to the edges.
  • Flag breakout level: the top (bull flag) or bottom (bear flag) of a consolidation. Well-defined and heavily stop-hunted.
  • Double-top neckline / double-bottom neckline: the horizontal trigger line that completes the pattern. Gets watched by everyone running the checklist on those tabs.
  • Round numbers (6,500 MES · 2,500 M2K): psychological magnets. Big orders cluster here regardless of chart structure.
Arnie's take I rank these in order of edge: PDH/PDL > ORB > round number > DT/DB neckline > flag break. When a failed breakout forms at PDH or PDL, that's when I size up. When it forms at a flag breakout that no one else is watching, I skip — the crowd isn't there, so there's no trap to spring.
3 · Six Setup Criteria ALL required
Criteria checked
0/6
ALL 6 REQUIRED
4 · Entry Trigger — The Reclaim Candle Close
Entry methodology
  • Trigger: close of the reclaim candle (bar closes back inside the prior range).
  • Fill: market order at the close, or stop order just on the reclaim-side of the candle body (small point buffer).
  • Do NOT anticipate: don't short just because price dipped below the level. Wait for the CLOSE.
Why "close, not touch" Intrabar price can cross and re-cross a level multiple times (wicks both ways). Only the close of the 2-min candle is committed market opinion. If you act on the touch, you'll get faked out half the time. If you act on the close, you've already seen the rejection be final for that bar.
5 · Stop Placement — Small Point Buffer Beyond the Failed Extreme
Stop math by contract (all values in points)
  • ES: $50/pt. Typical stop = 4–10 pts = $200–$500 per contract.
  • MES: $5/pt. Typical stop = 4–10 pts = $20–$50 per contract.
  • NQ: $20/pt. Typical stop = 5–12 pts = $100–$240 per contract.
  • MNQ: $2/pt. Typical stop = 5–12 pts = $10–$24 per contract.
  • RTY: $50/pt. Typical stop = 1.0–2.0 pts = $50–$100 per contract.
  • M2K: $5/pt. Typical stop = 1.0–2.0 pts = $5–$10 per contract.
Why "fail extreme," not reclaim extreme If the failed breakout is real, price should never revisit the failed extreme. If it does, your thesis is invalidated — the original direction is back in charge. That's the natural invalidation point and therefore the natural stop. Putting the stop closer than that gets you chopped out on noise; putting it farther than that wastes R.
6 · Target Management — T1 = 1.5-2R · T2 = Runner
Management sequence
  • At entry: bracket with full stop + T1 (opposite side of broken range) + T2 (runner).
  • At T1 (1.5–2R): take 50%, move stop to break-even. You are now risking zero.
  • At T2: trail stop behind swing structure. Exit on close through the trailing stop.
Why "50% at T1" and not all-or-nothing Failed breakouts reverse back into the range about 65% of the time, but they only run to the opposite extreme (T2) about 35% of the time. Taking half at T1 guarantees you book the high-probability win; letting the rest run lets you catch the occasional 3-5R home run without giving back the base case.
7 · Kill Switches — When To Skip
The five kill switches in plain English
  • Insignificant level: breakout wasn't at PDH/PDL/ORB/flag/DT-neckline/round number → no crowd trapped → no fuel.
  • Higher timeframe in strong opposing trend: the 5-min and 15-min are ripping up hard → your "failed breakout down" is probably just a pullback before more up.
  • Weak reclaim candle: doji, tiny body, long wicks both sides → no committed rejection. Needs a forceful close back inside.
  • News-driven spike: CPI, FOMC, NFP, or any major release within 30 min → bar volatility is random, not pattern-driven.
  • Stale setup: more than 5 candles (10 minutes) have passed since the reclaim → trap has dissipated, stops are gone, edge is gone.
8 · MES vs M2K — Character Differences
9 · Arnie's Approach
How I'll actually trade this live
  • Mark the five levels before the open: PDH, PDL, yesterday's close, key round numbers (6,500 MES · 2,500 M2K), and any prior-week extremes.
  • Mark the ORB range at 9:45 AM EST: high and low of 9:30–9:45. Add to the "levels to watch" list.
  • Wait for a breakout attempt, then sit on hands: do NOT trade the breakout. Watch it.
  • When price slips back through within 2–4 candles: count bars. If yes, flip to "reclaim watch."
  • Only pull the trigger on a full close back inside: no anticipation. Close or skip.
  • Size based on the 1R risk: 1% of account max. If stop is 8 MES pts ($40), my 1% allows me to size appropriately for $40 × contracts.
  • Bank half at T1, move to BE, runner to T2: mechanical management. No hero stuff.
  • Cap at 3 attempts per day: if I miss 3, I'm done. No chasing.
Why this is my A+ setup Because it passes every filter I care about: (1) I'm reacting, not predicting, (2) the level is significant and watchable, (3) the math is clean (tight stop, 1.5-2R T1, runner optionality), (4) it has a psychological tailwind (I'm patient while the crowd is chasing), (5) it's frequent enough to be worth hunting but rare enough to respect, (6) it works just as well at 10 AM as at 3 PM.
🌅 Opening Range Breakout with Retest (ORB-R)
□→←□
★ The Best Morning Setup
The opening 15 minutes define the day's initial range. When price breaks out and then retests the broken edge, you get a clean, mechanical, early-session trade with 60–65% win rate at 2:1 R:R (TradeDisciple tracked 64% across 800+ ES/NQ signals — same patterns on MES which tracks ES 1:1). The key discipline: do not enter on the initial break. Wait for the retest.
1 · What Is the Opening Range?
Definition
  • Timing: 9:30–9:45 AM EST (the first 15 minutes of the NYSE regular session).
  • Marks: the highest high and the lowest low printed during that 15 minutes.
  • Purpose: the opening range reflects overnight positioning meeting the first wave of RTH liquidity. Whichever side breaks first is typically meaningful.
Why this window specifically The first 15 minutes are when the biggest players finish putting on (or taking off) overnight exposure. The high and low printed in that window represent the current equilibrium between overnight buyers and the first wave of regular-hours traders. Breaks of that equilibrium are significant because they mean a new player stepped in with enough size to push past where the initial fight ended. The retest then confirms whether that new buyer is sticking around — and that's your trade.
2 · Six Setup Criteria ALL required
Criteria checked
0/6
ALL 6 REQUIRED
3 · Execution — Entry, Stop, Three Targets
Execution details (for LONG — flip for SHORT)
  • Entry: close of the retest reversal candle (pin bar / engulfing / inside bar at ORB High).
  • Stop: a small point buffer below the retest low (for long) or above the retest high (for short). Typical distance: 4–8 MES pts.
  • T1 = 1:1 R:R: take 50% off, move stop to break-even.
  • T2 = 1:2 R:R: base case — take another 25%.
  • T3 = 1:3+ R:R: runner — trail a stop behind 2-min swing lows (for long) or highs (for short). Exit on close through the trailing stop.
4 · Kill Switches — When To Skip the ORB-R Entirely
Kill switches in plain English
  • Gap open > 20 MES pts: range is already too wide, T2 would require a massive extension.
  • Holiday or half-day session: institutions are absent, volume is thin, patterns don't hold.
  • FOMC · CPI · NFP day: event-driven, pre-release chop + post-release spike both kill the setup.
  • Retest takes > 20 minutes: the trapped-breakout crowd has already exited; the edge is gone.
  • Chasing: if price is already halfway to T2 when you notice the retest, skip — you've missed the R:R.
Frequency reality check This setup is valid maybe 60–70% of trading days. That means 30–40% of days you simply do not take an ORB-R. That's the discipline. One clean ORB-R per day is the ceiling — there's no such thing as two.
5 · Arnie's Approach
How I'll actually trade this live
  • 9:30–9:45 EST: do NOT trade, watch only. Patience during this window is the whole edge.
  • At 9:45 sharp: lock in ORB High and ORB Low on the chart.
  • Scan for kill switches first: is there FOMC/CPI/NFP today? Is the ORB range >20 pts? Is it a half-day? If yes, close the setup and go to Clock Matrix / Failed Breakout tab instead.
  • Wait for a 2-min close outside the range: don't act. Just note it and start the retest stopwatch.
  • Within 20 minutes, watch for pullback to the broken line: if it gets there, look for pin / engulfing / inside bar.
  • Close of the rejection candle = bracket order: entry + stop (small point buffer below retest low for long) + T1 (1R) + T2 (2R) + runner to T3.
  • Strict one-and-done: max 1 ORB-R per day. If it stops out, I'm done with this setup for today — no "second attempt."
🌀 VWAP Mean Reversion with RSI · Range Play · 55–60% Win Rate
⇋
★ What this is — in plain English: Price has wandered too far from its session average (VWAP). Statistically, it usually comes back. When RSI(14) also shows the move is exhausted (>70 at a top, <30 at a bottom), and a rejection candle prints at the outer Bollinger/2nd std-dev band, the odds favor a fade back to VWAP. This is a range-play setup — works best when the market is chopping, not trending hard. Runs in the midday window: 11:30 AM–2:00 PM EST. 3–6 setups per day typical. 1.5:1 R:R. Skip entirely in strong trends, during news, or before the close.
1 · The Three Tools You Need on the Chart
VWAP (Volume-Weighted Average Price) — a running average that weights every price by its volume. It is the institutional fair-value benchmark. Mutual funds, pension funds, and algos measure their own execution against VWAP. This means there are real buyers just below VWAP and real sellers just above VWAP — it acts as a magnet and a dividing line.

Bollinger Bands (20-period, 2 std dev) — or use a volume-weighted equivalent. The upper and lower bands mark where price is two standard deviations away from the 20-period mean. By definition, price spends ~95% of the time inside these bands. A close at or beyond a band is a statistical extreme — the move is likely overextended.

RSI(14) — Relative Strength Index, 14-period. Measures momentum on a 0–100 scale. Above 70 = overbought. Below 30 = oversold. By itself, RSI is weak — trends ride at 75+ for hours. But combined with a touch of the 2nd std dev band and a rejection candle, it becomes a high-confluence exhaustion signal.
2 · The 5-Criteria Setup Checklist (all must be true)
1
Market is ranging, not trending hard. ADX < 25 ideal. If the 20-EMA is climbing/falling at a steep angle, mean reversion fails — trend wins. Visual rule: if the last 10 candles make a clean stairstep, SKIP.
2
Price is AT or just BEYOND the 2nd std dev band. Candle body or wick touches the band. Not "near." Not "almost." Touching or breaching.
3
RSI(14) is >70 for shorts (upper band) or <30 for longs (lower band). Confirms momentum exhaustion. Even better: RSI is rolling over (just crossed back under 70 / back over 30).
4
A rejection candle prints. Pin bar (long wick into the band, small body), bearish/bullish engulfing, or shooting star/hammer at the extreme. The candle must CLOSE back inside the bands.
5
Time of day is midday: 11:30 AM – 2:00 PM EST. This is when trends exhaust, volume drops, and ranges form. Do NOT run this setup in the first 90 minutes (9:30–11:00) — trends dominate. Do NOT run in the last 30 minutes — closing auctions distort.
3 · Execution — Entry, Stop, Target
Entry (SHORT from upper band):On the close of the rejection candle back inside the bands. Enter at the open of the NEXT bar.
Entry (LONG from lower band):On the close of the rejection candle (hammer/bullish engulfing) back inside the bands. Enter at the open of the NEXT bar.
Stop:A small point buffer beyond the rejection candle's extreme (above the high for shorts, below the low for longs). Per-instrument: 1.0 pt ES/MES, 1.5 pt MNQ, 2.0 pt NQ, 0.4 pt RTY/M2K.
Target 1 (primary):VWAP. This is where 70%+ of mean-reversion trades resolve. Take 1/2 to 2/3 of position off.
Target 2 (runner):Opposite band if price has clear range structure (bouncing between bands all day). Trail the rest of the position with the 20-EMA.
R:R:1.5:1 minimum. Don't accept less. If VWAP is only 1R away from entry, the trade is too tight — skip.
4 · Kill Switches — When to Skip This Setup Entirely
🚫 Strong trend day. If the 9 or 20-EMA is sloped steeply and price is making clean higher highs/lows (or lower highs/lows), this setup gets crushed. Trend trumps mean reversion. Check: is ADX(14) > 25? Is price pulling away from VWAP repeatedly? If yes, SKIP.
🚫 News window. FOMC (2 PM EST Wed of meeting week), CPI, NFP (1st Fri of month), PCE, jobless claims — news spikes blow past bands and keep going. Check the economic calendar before placing any trade. If a tier-1 release is within 30 minutes, SKIP.
🚫 Wide ATR / high volatility day. If ATR is 2x normal, the 2nd std dev band is also 2x normal — you need a much bigger stop for the same mean reversion geometry, and R:R breaks down. Check 14-period ATR vs. 30-day average.
🚫 Near session open (before 11:00 EST) or close (after 2:00 EST). Opening drive and closing auction distort mean reversion. The midday window (11:30–2:00) is the sweet spot. Religiously enforce.
🚫 Already took the opposite setup on the same day. If you already shorted the upper band and hit target, and now price is at the lower band tempting a long — pause. Two trades on the same pattern is fine, but three+ fading is revenge trading in disguise. Max 3 VWAP MR trades per session.
5 · Arnie's Approach — Live Trading Process for MES & M2K
1
