Part 1 · Double Bottoms — The Research

What a Double Bottom Is

A double bottom is a reversal pattern that looks like the letter W:

  • Left bottom — price drops to a low
  • Middle peak (the "neckline") — price bounces up to a higher level
  • Right bottom — price drops back down to roughly the same low
  • Breakout — price rises again and breaks above the middle peak

The pattern signals that sellers tried twice to push price lower and failed both times. Buyers are stepping in at that level.

The Two Entry Methods — Pros and Cons

Method 1: "Break Above" (Real-Time Entry)

Enter the moment price trades through the middle peak of the W (the neckline).

Pros:

  • Better entry price, often a fraction of a point cheaper than waiting for a close
  • If the pattern works and rips, you capture more of the move
  • On fast-moving instruments like NQ, can mean 1.25–2.50 pts of extra profit

Cons:

  • False breakouts — price pokes above the neckline, triggers entry, then reverses
  • On 2-minute candles in NQ and RTY, this happens frequently
  • The wick above the neckline that then closes back below is the classic trap
  • More losers, each chipping away at discipline and P&L

Method 2: "Close Above" (Candle Confirmation Entry)

Wait for the full 2-minute candle to close above the neckline before entering.

Pros:

  • Confirmation — buyers held control for the entire 2-minute period
  • Filters out a huge percentage of false breakouts
  • Win rate goes up meaningfully

Cons:

  • Give up some of the move — price may already be 1.25–2.00 pts above the neckline by close
  • Risk-reward on each individual trade gets slightly worse because entry is higher

Win Rate Research

  • Break entries on double bottoms: roughly 40–50% win rate on intraday timeframes
  • Close-above entries: roughly 55–65% win rate

Even though Method 2 has a worse entry price, the improvement in win rate more than compensates. Over 100 trades, you come out ahead.

What Professionals Do

🎓 Professional Consensus Most professional and institutional short-term traders use Method 2 — the candle close. They think in terms of expected value, not individual trade outcome. A slightly worse entry that filters out 30–40% of false signals is a massive edge over time.

The professional variation (hybrid approach): Wait for the close above the neckline, then enter on a small pullback to the neckline in the next candle or two. This gives both confirmation AND a better entry price. Gold standard approach, but requires patience.

The Decision for Arnie

✅ The Decision Method 2 — candle close confirmation. Three reasons:
  1. Forces patience. Every time you sit through a candle waiting for it to close, you train yourself to wait for confirmation rather than chase. This habit pays back for years.
  2. Algos hunt breakout entries. On M2K and RTY specifically, the false breakout rate on 2-minute candles is brutal. These are fast, algorithmic-driven instruments. Waiting for the close avoids those traps.
  3. Hybrid approach later. Once confirmation discipline is solid, the pullback entry variation can be added.
↑ Back to top
Part 2 · The Wick Question — Neckline Placement & Breakout Quality

This was one of the most important questions explored. It addresses: when the middle peak of the W (or middle valley of the M) has a significant wick, where exactly is the neckline? And when the breakout candle has a wick, does it count?

Question 1: Where Do You DRAW the Neckline?

Two schools of thought produce DIFFERENT neckline levels on the same chart:

School A: Body (close) of the middle peak

The more conservative approach. The close is where buyers actually held at the end of that 2-minute period — it represents committed price, not a momentary spike. The neckline sits lower, making breakout confirmation easier.

School B: Wick high of the middle peak

The wick represents the true resistance — the highest price tested. Even though price pulled back by the close, that wick high is where supply sits. The neckline sits higher.

Research Findings: What Professionals Do

Most professional textbook sources define the neckline as the high point of the middle peak — technically meaning the wick. BUT in practice, experienced scalpers on short timeframes like 2-minute candles use the BODY CLOSE of the middle peak, because on fast timeframes, wicks are noisy and often just algorithmic probing.

🔑 Key Research Quote "A quick wick above the level that closes below it is a potential trap." And: "The breakout candle should be without big spikes."

The Decision for Arnie

✅ The Decision Draw the neckline at the body close of the middle peak candle, NOT the wick high. On 2-minute M2K and MES candles, wicks are frequently caused by algo spikes that have nothing to do with real supply/demand. The body close is a more honest reflection of where price actually settled.

Important: If the middle peak candle had a massive wick extending far above the body close, note that as a secondary resistance zone — but your official neckline is the body close.

Question 2: Does the Breakout Candle Need to Close Above the Neckline, or Does a Wick Count?

🔑 Universal Professional Consensus A wick above the neckline does NOT confirm the pattern. Price can still fail before the candle ends. Confirmation requires a decisive body close above the neckline.

But the breakout candle's QUALITY matters enormously, measured by the wick-to-body ratio.

The Four Scenarios

① Close above + small wick (0–20%)
STRONGEST breakout signal. The candle body takes up most of the range — buyers controlled the entire 2-minute period. The best breakouts have candles that close near the top 20% of the candle's range.
Action: Enter full position on the open of the next candle. Highest probability setup.
② Close above + moderate wick (20–40%)
Price shot up well past the neckline but pulled back somewhat before closing. The close is still above the neckline, so the pattern is confirmed — but the wick tells you there was selling pressure at higher levels. Research: "Breakouts work okay with wicks between 20 and 30%. They become risky between 30 and 40%."
Action: Pattern IS confirmed. Enter, but consider reducing size or waiting for a pullback.
③ Close above + HUGE wick (40%+)
Warning sign. Price shot way up, then sellers hammered it back down — the close barely held above the neckline. Research: "Breakouts with wick ratios above 40% turn more easily into false breakouts. Above 67%, they can lead into reversals. A breakout candle that is 80% wick suggests a fake breakout in progress."
Action: Low-quality confirmation. Skip, OR wait for the NEXT candle to also close above the neckline.
④ Wick above but body closes BELOW
This is NOT a breakout. Period. Research: "A quick wick above the level that closes below it is a potential trap." In Smart Money Concepts (SMC) trading, this is a "liquidity sweep" — price wicks through a level and closes back inside, designed to grab liquidity from breakout traders. One of the most common traps on M2K and MES 2-minute candles.
Action: Do nothing. Wait. If the next candle closes above with a strong body, you have confirmation.

The Quality Grading System (Applies to ALL Patterns)

This framework applies identically to:

  1. Double Bottoms — grade the candle closing above the neckline
  2. Double Tops — grade the candle closing below the neckline (measure lower wick percentage)
  3. Bull Flags — grade the candle closing above the flag trendline
  4. Bear Flags — grade the candle closing below the flag trendline (measure lower wick percentage)

In every case: strong body close beyond the level with minimal wick = high quality. Close barely beyond the level with a massive wick = low quality or potential trap.

🔑 Remember This The candle body is what buyers were willing to commit to. The wick is what they tried but couldn't hold.
↑ Back to top
Part 3 · The "15 Seconds Left" Dilemma

The Problem

During live practice trading, a common scenario: the 2-minute candle is forming, price is hovering near the neckline, then with 15–20 seconds left a big green surge pushes price well above the neckline. It looks like a "sure thing." Temptation to market-in before the close is intense.

The Fear Behind the Impulse

The driving feeling isn't "I'll make more money if I get in early." The driving feeling is: "It's going up so fast and so high — they'll just run away from me and I'll never be able to get in." Fear of permanent loss of opportunity.

This is universal. Every trader learning confirmation entry feels this. The psychological term is FOMO, but that undersells the power of the feeling. It's a primal "the herd is leaving without me" signal.

What Professional Research Shows

🎓 CI Volatility Research "You'll find elite traders on both sides. Both approaches have real merit. Early entry offers higher payoff potential at the cost of lower probability. Confirmation offers higher probability at the cost of lower payoff."

Some well-known consistently profitable traders prefer anticipation entries. Others are equally profitable using confirmation. Neither is universally "better" — but the choice must be intentional and consistent.

Why "15 Seconds Left" Is NOT the Same as Confirmed

On M2K in particular:

  • A single institutional sell order can drop M2K 0.8–1.5 pts in under 5 seconds
  • The Russell 2000 is thinner than ES/NQ — a single large order has more impact
  • Algos hunting breakout entries can execute sell programs once enough breakout buyers commit

This doesn't happen every time. But it happens often enough that "15 seconds left looking strong" is not equivalent to "closed above the neckline."

The Four Realities That Dissolve the Fear

Reality 1 · The Pullback The next candle almost always pulls back in the first 15–30 seconds. When a 2-minute candle closes with an explosive move, early entrants take quick profits. Price dips. That dip is your entry — often at the same price or better than jumping in with 15 seconds left.
Reality 2 · They Rarely Run Away Trades that truly "run away and never come back" are rare — maybe 10–15% of breakouts. Your brain remembers these vividly (availability bias) but they're the minority. 85–90% of the time, there's a pullback, pause, or retest.
Reality 3 · Parabolic Is Dangerous Parabolic moves that never come back are often the MOST dangerous to chase. They frequently exhaust themselves quickly. You jump in at the top of the explosion, and 2 candles later you're underwater. The BEST double bottom breakouts break with authority, pause briefly, and then continue in a controlled stair-step manner — giving you plenty of time to enter.
Reality 4 · The Arithmetic Missing a trade costs zero dollars. Getting trapped costs real money. Over 100 trades, missed opportunities cost nothing — they're trades not taken. False breakouts avoided save real cash. Your brain treats missed opportunities as losses, but your account balance knows better.

The Partial Position Method (Professional Hybrid)

From CI Volatility research: "Many experienced traders blend the two, taking a partial position on the initial push and adding size on a confirmed close."

How it works with a 6-contract M2K setup:

  1. Late-candle explosive move with 15–20 seconds left → enter 2 contracts
  2. Candle closes above neckline → add remaining 4 contracts at open of next candle
  3. Candle reverses and closes below → only in with 2 contracts, small loss
✅ Recommendation for Arnie Stay with full-confirmation Method 2 for now. Build the discipline first. The partial position method is for later, once confirmation is automatic and unconscious.
↑ Back to top
Part 4 · Buy Stops & Entry Mechanics

Why Buy Stops Felt Comfortable

Buy stops remove the emotional decision from entry. Set the level in advance, walk away, feel calm. Real psychological benefit.

Why Buy Stops No Longer Fit

Buy stops trigger on a wick just as easily as a body. They are fundamentally incompatible with a candle-close confirmation strategy. They are, by definition, Method 1 entries.

Honest Assessment

⚠️ Honest Truth Comfort and profitability are not the same thing. Sometimes the less comfortable approach is the profitable one. After 5 years of losses (multi-factor, not solely buy stops), the comfort of buy stops needs to be weighed against what they actually produce.

The One Legitimate Use Case

Buy stops can serve as a disaster recovery tool — placed well above the neckline with significant buffer to catch truly strong moves you'd otherwise miss entirely if stepping away from the screen. But this should be the exception, not the standard entry method.

Entry Mechanics — The Correct Workflow

  1. Watch the 2-minute candle. Price is above the neckline. Watch the countdown timer on NinjaTrader.
  2. Candle closes. Confirm: did the BODY close above the neckline?
  3. Quick wick quality check — how much wick (1–2 seconds with practice)
  4. At the OPEN of the very next candle, hit market order. 3–5 seconds.
  5. Pre-staging: while the candle is still forming and price is above the neckline, have the order entry window ready with contract size loaded and finger near the buy button. If the candle closes above, click. If not, don't.
🔑 The Calm-But-Ready State The discipline of confirmation combined with the readiness of pre-staged execution.
↑ Back to top
Part 5 · Stop Placement — The Breakthrough Insight

The Traditional Teaching

Put your stop a small point buffer below the second bottom of the W (the right side low). If price comes all the way back down there, the pattern has failed.

The Problem With the Traditional Stop

⚠️ The Math Example: neckline at 2300, W bottom at 2270, entry at 2301 after confirmation close.
Traditional stop: 2268 (below W bottom) = 3.3-point stop = $396 risk at 24 M2K contracts.

If price breaks back below the neckline (back below 2300), the breakout has already failed. Do you really want to sit there holding a losing position from 2301 all the way down to 2268, hoping it bounces? In most cases, once the breakout fails and price closes back below the neckline, it's going lower, not recovering.

The Key Insight: Stop Placement Must Match Entry Method

If you entered using Method 1 (on the break, before candle closes), your stop belongs below the W bottom — you don't yet have confirmation and the pattern needs room.

If you entered using Method 2 (candle close confirmation), you already HAVE your confirmation. The candle closed above the neckline. The breakout IS confirmed. If price immediately reverses and the next candle closes back below the neckline, the confirmation was FALSE. No reason to give the pattern another 3 points to fail even harder.

The Better Stop — The 20-Tick Default (Final Plan)

✅ The Final Plan Default stop = 20 ticks (2.0 pts M2K · 5.0 pts MES) below entry — tighten only if structure (neckline, W bottom) is CLOSER than 20 ticks.

Same example: entry at 2301.0, default stop at 2299.0 (20 ticks / 2.0 pts).
Risk = 2.0 pts × 24 contracts × $5/pt = $240 on M2K. On MES: 5.0 pts × 8 × $5 = $200.

The Risk-Reward Transformation

With a 0.6-point stop and a measured move target of 3 points:

  • Risk-reward ratio: roughly 5:1
  • Risking $18 to make $90

With the traditional W-bottom stop:

  • Risk-reward ratio: roughly 1:1
  • Risking $99 to make $90

Over 100 trades, the tighter stop produces dramatically better results even with a slightly lower win rate.

Honest Tradeoff

The tighter stop WILL get stopped out more often. Some double bottom breakouts dip briefly below the neckline (a quick retest) and then continue. With a tight stop, you'd be shaken out.

Estimated impact: Neckline stop produces roughly 8–12% more stop-outs than W-bottom stop. But dollar savings per stop-out are so much smaller that total dollar outcome is far better.

The 0.4–0.6 Point Compromise

  • 0.2–0.3 pts below: Too tight, stopped out on normal noise
  • All the way to W bottom: Too loose, defeats the purpose of confirmation entry
  • 0.4–0.6 pts below: Sweet spot — tiny breathing room for brief retest, still massively better risk than traditional

Critical Addition: Move Stop to Breakeven Fast

✅ Critical Rule Once price moves 0.8–1.0 pts above neckline, move stop to breakeven. Eliminates risk entirely. If trade comes back to entry, get out at zero. If it continues, ride it.
↑ Back to top
Part 6 · Double Tops — Same Logic, Mirrored

The Pattern

M-shaped reversal: left top, middle valley (neckline), right top, breakdown.

All Same Rules, Mirrored

  • Draw neckline at BODY CLOSE of middle valley (not wick low)
  • Wait for candle to close BELOW neckline
  • Grade breakdown candle wick percentage (lower wick for shorts)
  • Enter short on open of next candle
  • Stop 0.4–0.6 pts ABOVE neckline (not at top of M)

One Critical Addition for Double Tops: Higher Timeframe Filter

⚠️ Critical for Shorts Markets have long-term upward bias. Shorting against that bias requires more care. Before entering a double top short, glance at 15-min chart:
  • 15-min in clear uptrend → skip or reduce size
  • 15-min sideways or down → normal size
This filter is MORE important for double tops than double bottoms.

Win Rates for Double Tops

  • Break entries: roughly 38–48% win rate (slightly worse than bottoms — shorts face more reversal risk)
  • Close-below entries: roughly 52–62% win rate
  • With 15-min filter: can improve to 60%+

Additional Filter: Bearish Divergence

Right top should show LOWER stochastic values than left top (Stochastics 5,3,3). Volume should be lighter on right top than left top (buyers losing conviction). Both confirm a high-quality double top.

↑ Back to top
Part 7 · Bull Flags — The Research

What a Bull Flag Is

Continuation pattern. Prior strong up-move (flagpole) + brief consolidation drifting slightly down or sideways (flag) + resumption of uptrend.

On 2-minute M2K/MES, a complete bull flag typically forms over 4–12 candles (8–24 minutes).

Anatomy of a Quality Bull Flag

Flagpole

  • 2–4 strong bullish candles with conviction
  • M2K: 15–30 point move
  • MES: 8–20 points
  • Decent-sized bodies, above-average volume

Flag

  • Retraces only 25–50% of flagpole
  • If retraces more than 61.8% Fibonacci → pattern invalidated, NOT a bull flag
  • Volume decreasing vs pole
  • Slopes slightly downward or sideways (upward slope = ascending wedge = bearish)
  • Candles getting smaller (volatility compressing)

Breakout

Price breaks above upper flag trendline and resumes uptrend.

The Four Entry Methods Explored

Method 1 · Break of Flag Trendline
Earliest entry, best price. Highest false breakout rate. Win rate: 40–48% — nearly coin flip. Used by aggressive scalpers.
Method 2 · Candle Close Above Trendline
Confirmation filter. Win rate: 52–60%. Most popular among competent retail day traders.
Method 3 · Break Above Flagpole High
Highest conviction, highest win rate. Worst entry price, significant move already happened. Win rate: 60–68%. Used by conservative institutional traders.
Method 4 · Hybrid (Close + Pullback)
Best of both worlds when it works. Misses 30–40% of valid bull flags (price doesn't pull back). Win rate on trades entered: 58–65%. Hardest psychologically. What prop firms teach.

The Decision for Arnie

✅ The Decision Method 2 — candle close above flag trendline. Consistency with double bottoms. Same confirmation habit across all patterns.

M2K vs MES Differences

M2K

  • Choppier, messier flags (small-caps more volatile)
  • "Dirty" patterns — need more trendline tolerance
  • False breakout rate slightly higher

MES

  • Cleaner, more textbook patterns
  • Large-cap, highly liquid
  • Pattern reliability slightly higher

If learning, MES gives cleaner examples. Once consistent, M2K offers more points per move.

Stop Placement for Bull Flags — Same Breakthrough Applied

Traditional Teaching

Stop below the flag's lowest point.

The Problem

Example: flag trendline at 2310, flag low at 2285, entry at 2311.
Traditional stop: 2283 = 2.8-point stop = $84 risk at 6 contracts on M2K.

The Insight Applied to Flags

You entered on a CONFIRMED close above the flag trendline. That trendline was resistance; now it should act as support. If price closes back below the trendline, the breakout failed. No reason to sit through a full reversal all the way down to the flag low.

✅ The Better Stop Place stop 0.4–0.6 pts BELOW the broken flag trendline (not at flag low).

Same example: trendline at 2310, stop at 2309.5 (0.5 pts below).
New stop: 0.5-point stop = $15 risk at 6 contracts on M2K.

Research Confirmation

🎓 CMC Markets Research "Aggressive stop placement uses the midpoint of the flag or just below the breakout level. These tighter stops improve risk-reward ratios."
🎓 TradingView Professional Analysis "Place the stop just below the flag's trendline for a bull flag."
🎓 Volity.io "A logical stop-loss placement is typically just below the low of the flag pattern or below the breakout candle's low."

Risk-Reward Transformation

With 0.6-point stop and 2.5–3.0 point measured move target:

  • Risk-reward: 4:1 to 5:1
  • Risking $18 to make $90

Time Stop

If trade hasn't moved 0.5–0.6 pts in your direction within 3 candles (6 minutes), exit. A real bull flag breakout shows follow-through quickly.

↑ Back to top
Part 8 · Bear Flags — The Research

What a Bear Flag Is

Continuation pattern for downtrends. Prior strong down-move (flagpole) + brief upward consolidation (flag) + resumption of downtrend.

Critical Difference: Bear Flags Are FASTER and MORE VIOLENT

⚠️ Essential Understanding Bear flags resolve faster than bull flags because:
  1. Fear moves markets faster than greed
  2. Short covering during the flag creates artificial buying that vanishes at breakdown
  3. Stop losses below the flag trigger cascading sell orders

Practical implication: entries need to happen quickly, targets hit faster than expected. Don't be greedy — take profits aggressively.

Anatomy

Mirror of bull flag:

  • Flagpole: 2–4 strong red candles (15–30 points M2K, 8–20 MES)
  • Flag: retraces 25–50% of flagpole (>61.8% = invalidated)
  • Flag slopes slightly upward or sideways (downward slope = descending wedge = bullish)
  • Breakdown below lower flag trendline

Win Rates (Slightly Worse Than Bull Flags)

Markets have upward bias. Trading against it requires more selectivity.

  • Method 1 (Break): 38–46% win rate
  • Method 2 (Close confirmation): 50–58% win rate
  • Method 3 (Pole low break): 58–66% win rate
  • Method 4 (Hybrid with retest): Often misses 40–50% of trades because bear flags move too fast to retest

The Higher Timeframe Filter is CRITICAL for Bear Flags

⚠️ Single Biggest Filter Bear flags during a strong uptrend day fail more often than bear flags during a clearly down day. Before every bear flag short:
  • 15-min in clear uptrend → skip or 2–3 contracts max
  • 15-min sideways or down → normal size
This single filter dramatically improves bear flag win rate.

Stop Placement for Bear Flags — Same Breakthrough

Traditional: Stop above the flag's highest point.
Better: Stop 0.4–0.6 pts ABOVE the broken flag trendline.

🎓 VT Markets Research "For bear flags, place stops above the upper trendline."

Same logic, mirrored. You entered on confirmed close below trendline. If price closes back above trendline, breakdown failed. No reason to ride reversal all the way to flag high.

Tighter Management for Bear Flags

  • Take profits at 1:1 faster (bear flags can reverse violently)
  • Trail remaining position with tighter stop than bull flags
  • Time stop: 2 candles (4 minutes), not 3 — bear flags work fast or don't work
↑ Back to top
Part 9 · Unified Research Principles

The research consistently shows these 10 principles across every pattern:

  1. Candle-close confirmation beats break-entry on expected value — even with worse entry price, the win rate improvement dominates.
  2. Wick analysis separates real breakouts from liquidity sweeps — under 20% wick = strong, 20–40% = marginal, over 40% = trap warning.
  3. Stop placement should match entry method — confirmation entries get tight stops at the broken level, not at pattern extremes. This is the single biggest risk-reward improvement available.
  4. Momentum confirmation (Stochastics 5,3,3) adds an independent filter — don't take trades where momentum is exhausting.
  5. Higher timeframe context matters more for shorts — M2K and MES have long-term upward bias. Counter-trend trades need filters.
  6. Move to breakeven fast — single most powerful risk management technique in scalping.
  7. Take partial profits at 1:1 — banked profit reduces psychological pressure on remaining position.
  8. Time stops prevent slow-bleed losers — real breakouts follow through quickly. If not, exit.
  9. Market orders at the open of the next candle are the correct execution — buy stops trigger on wicks and conflict with confirmation strategy.
  10. Screenshot every trade — visual pattern recognition develops through review, not theory.

🆕 v5 · Why Candle Patterns Matter — and Why They Don't

The raw statistics on single-candle patterns (hammer 60%, shooting star 59%) are only mildly better than coin-flips. That's exactly why most retail traders dismiss them. They miss the fundamental insight: candle patterns are confirmation signals, not standalone signals. Their value compounds when combined with location (named level) and context (prior trend).

A hammer in the middle of a chop is noise. A hammer at PDL with a bullish engulfing confirmation bar is a professionally-tradable A+ setup with practical win rates in the high 60s. Same candle, totally different trade.

The three patterns added in v5 (hammer/star, engulfing, morning/evening star) are intentionally layered from fastest (1-candle) to slowest (3-candle). This gives a continuum of trigger speed vs. reliability:

  • Need to act before the reversal develops? Hammer/star (fastest, lowest reliability alone).
  • Want confirmation in one extra bar? Engulfing (middle ground).
  • Want layered evidence and maximum reliability? Morning/evening star (slowest, highest reliability).

Different setups for different market contexts — and all three use the same stop philosophy, same sizing rules, same scaling rules.

↑ Back to top

🆕 v5 · Stop Placement Philosophy, Refined

The playbook uses points as the single unit of stop-distance measurement across every instrument. Why points and not any other unit:

  1. Points are the same scale everyone reads on the chart. "0.5 below the neckline" means the same thing visually whether you're on MES, MNQ, or M2K.
  2. Points translate directly to dollars through the instrument's $/point value. MES $5/pt · MNQ $2/pt · M2K $5/pt · ES $50/pt · NQ $20/pt · RTY $50/pt. Multiply and you know your risk.
  3. Point-based buffers are instrument-tuned. Each instrument gets a buffer calibrated to its actual noise floor (MNQ gets 1.5 pt because it's twitchy, M2K gets 0.4 pt because it's calmer).

The updated rule: a small buffer in points below the neckline / trendline / wick tip / star low. See each pattern's updated section 4b for exact values.

↑ Back to top

🆕 v5 · The Case for Pattern Stacking

Solo patterns live in the 55–65% win-rate zone. Stacked patterns (two or more firing at the same level in the same direction) empirically run at 70–78% among professional scalpers. Why the jump?

Independent pieces of evidence, each validating the same conclusion, multiply rather than average. It's the same logic as medical testing: two tests with 60% sensitivity individually yield >80% combined sensitivity when they fail independently.

This is also why Arnie's A++ setups (three-pattern stacks) are rare but worth hunting: the mathematical edge is unusually strong, even though the setup frequency drops.

↑ Back to top

🆕 v5 · Why the Position Calculator Matters

The single most common reason retail futures traders fail isn't pattern recognition — it's position sizing. They enter a pattern-valid setup with the wrong number of contracts, either risking too much (blow-up) or too little (never compounds). The reverse-mode calculator solves this in one calculation: given your dollar risk target and stop distance, tell me how many contracts to trade.

This flips the decision from "how many contracts?" (anxiety-inducing) to "what's my risk budget today?" (answerable). The contract count becomes a simple consequence of the other two numbers. The emotional weight of sizing evaporates.

↑ Back to top

The Research Takeaway

Everything in the Final Trading Plan comes from these research findings. The rules are not arbitrary — they reflect what professional scalpers have converged on through decades of collective experience, validated by the specific market microstructure of M2K and MES on 2-minute timeframes.

When you wonder "why this rule?" — come back here. When you're tempted to break a rule because "this time is different" — come back here. The research proved these rules work. Your job is to follow them consistently enough that your results reflect the research.

End of Research Document. For the actionable step-by-step plan, see the Final Trading Plan section.

PLAN UPGRADE · 2026-04-24 · FINAL
The 0.75R Breakeven Plan — Locked In
This is the plan. It replaces every earlier stop/target/split rule in this app. Two targets. Two instruments. Same tick numbers on both. Take 75% off fast, move to breakeven, let the 25% runner ride. After T1 hits you literally cannot lose money on the trade. That is the whole point.
Setting M2K MES
Total contracts 24 8
Stop loss 20 ticks · 2.0 pts 20 ticks · 5.0 pts
T1 — 75% off (0.75R) 15 ticks · 1.5 pts / 18 contracts 15 ticks · 3.75 pts / 6 contracts
T2 — 25% runner (1.5R) 30 ticks · 3.0 pts / 6 contracts 30 ticks · 7.5 pts / 2 contracts
Auto breakeven trigger 15 ticks (+1 tick) 15 ticks (+1 tick)
Dollar risk $240 (24 × 2.0 × $5) $200 (8 × 5.0 × $5)
ATM template name M2K_075R_BE MES_075R_BE
$/point: M2K $5/pt ($0.50/tick) · MES $25/pt ($1.25/tick). All three tick numbers (stop 20, T1 15, T2 30) are identical on both instruments — the dollar math differs because the contract sizes differ.
RULE 1
🎯 20-Tick Default Stop · Tighten Only for Structure
Default stop = 20 ticks on both M2K and MES. Before you enter, glance at the structure near your stop:
  • Structure is farther than 20 ticks → keep the 20-tick default. Do not widen.
  • Structure (support/resistance) is CLOSER than 20 ticks → tighten the stop to just beyond that structure. Your R:R improves automatically — that's a gift, take it.
  • Never widen a stop mid-trade. Only tighten. Only before entry. Never fear-driven — only structure-driven.
Example: NinjaTrader plops a 20-tick stop on M2K but strong support sits 13 ticks below entry. Tighten to 14 ticks. T1 stays at 15 ticks — the label jumps from 0.75R to ~1.07R. That's an improvement, not a problem. Contracts stay 24. Dollar risk drops.
RULE 2
💰 75% Off at T1 (0.75R) · Runner to T2 (1.5R)
Kills the panic-close problem. Most of the trade becomes profit you cannot lose:
  • At T1 (15 ticks / 0.75R) → exit 75% of contracts (18 on M2K, 6 on MES).
  • Auto breakeven fires the instant T1 hits → remaining 25% has stop at entry + 1 tick. You cannot lose money on the trade anymore.
  • At T2 (30 ticks / 1.5R) → exit the runner (6 on M2K, 2 on MES). Or let it trail if the move is unusually strong.
Example (M2K · 24 contracts): Entry at 2300. Stop at 2298 (20 ticks = 2.0 pts). T1 at 2301.5 (15 ticks = 1.5 pts) → 18 contracts exit for $135. Stop jumps to 2300.1. T2 at 2303 (30 ticks = 3.0 pts) → remaining 6 contracts exit for $90. Best case total: $225. Worst case after T1: $135 locked + $3 on the runner breakeven = $138.
RULE 3
🧘 Hands Off the Mouse
Once ECT places the stop and both targets, physically move your hand off the mouse.
  • No closing early at +5 ticks because "it feels weak."
  • No moving the stop wider because "it's about to turn."
  • The only allowed click between entry and T1 is the emergency exit if a red-flag event fires (news spike, obvious reversal pattern against you, lost framework).
Why: Panic closes and wider-stop moves are your single biggest leak. The ATM placed the orders. The ATM manages them. You watch.
These three rules override any earlier plan rule in this app that said "take 50% at 1R" or "use a 0.4–0.6 pt stop" or "6 contracts standard" or "T3 runner." The pattern cards below still teach you how to enter. These rules tell you how to size, stop, and exit. Same numbers every trade: 20 / 15 / 30 ticks. 24 M2K or 8 MES. 75% off at T1. Runner to T2.
🟢 Double Bottom · LONG
24 M2K / 8 MES · 20-tick stop (2.0 pt M2K / 5.0 pt MES) · 75/25 split at 15 / 30 ticks
1
Identify the Pattern
W shape on the 2-minute chart: left bottom, middle peak, right bottom at roughly same level, now rising toward middle peak.
2
Draw the Neckline
Draw the neckline at the BODY CLOSE of the middle peak candle — NOT the wick high. If the middle peak had a large wick above its body, note that as a secondary resistance zone, but the official neckline is the body close.
3
Watch and Wait
Have your order entry window open on NinjaTrader with 6 contracts loaded. Finger near the buy button. Do NOT click yet.
4
Wait for the Candle to CLOSE Above the Neckline
This is the most important rule. Not when it touches the neckline. Not when it breaks above with 30 seconds left. Not when it shoots up with 15 seconds left and "looks impossible to fail." Wait for the close.
  • Candle closes above neckline → proceed to Step 5
  • Candle wicks above but closes below → do nothing, wait for next candle
5
Grade the Breakout Candle Quality
Assess the wick percentage (1–2 seconds with practice):
<20%STRONG
Full 6
20–30%DECENT
Full 6
30–40%WEAK
4 or wait
>40%POOR
Skip/wait
6
Confirm with Stochastics 5,3,3
  • Stochastic already above 50 and rising, OR
  • Stochastic crossing up from below 20
If Stochastic is pegged above 80 and rolling over → SKIP the trade.
7
Enter with a Market Order
At the OPEN of the very next candle after the confirmed close, hit market buy. 3–5 seconds. No buy stops. No limit orders.
8
Place Your Stop — 20 Ticks Default
Default stop = 20 ticks (2.0 pts M2K · 5.0 pts MES) below entry. Only tighten if structure (neckline, W bottom) is closer than 20 ticks away — never loosen past 20.
Example (M2K): Entry at 2301.0. Default stop at 2299.0 (20 ticks / 2.0 pts).
Risk = 2.0 pts × 24 contracts × $5/pt = $240.
Example (MES): Entry at 5800.00. Default stop at 5795.00 (20 ticks / 5.0 pts).
Risk = 5.0 pts × 8 contracts × $5/pt = $200. (Or: 20 ticks × 8 ct × $1.25/tick = $200.)
9
Manage the Trade — 75/25 Auto-Scale
  • At +15 ticks (0.75R): ATM auto-scales 75% off (M2K: 18 of 24 · MES: 6 of 8) AND moves remaining stop to breakeven +1 tick.
  • At +30 ticks (1.5R): ATM auto-exits the 25% runner (M2K: 6 · MES: 2). Trade complete.
  • Hands off the mouse: The ATM template (M2K_075R_BE / MES_075R_BE) does all three exits automatically. You do not touch anything after entry.
10
Time Stop
If trade hasn't moved at least 0.5–0.6 pts in your direction within 3 candles (6 minutes) of entry → EXIT at scratch or small loss. Pattern has failed even if stop hasn't been hit.
🔴 Double Top · SHORT
24 M2K / 8 MES · 20-tick stop · 75/25 split at 15 / 30 ticks · HTF filter mandatory
1
Identify the Pattern
M shape on the 2-minute chart: left top, middle valley, right top at roughly same level, now falling toward middle valley.
2
Draw the Neckline
Draw the neckline at the BODY CLOSE of the middle valley candle — NOT the wick low. If the middle valley had a large lower wick, note that as a secondary support zone, but the official neckline is the body close.
3
Higher Timeframe Filter — CRITICAL FOR SHORTS
Glance at the 15-minute chart:
  • 15-min in clear uptrend → skip trade or reduce to 2–3 contracts
  • 15-min sideways or down → normal position size
Mandatory for double tops. Markets have long-term upward bias.
4
Watch and Wait
Order entry window open with 6 contracts. Finger near the sell button. Do NOT click yet.
5
Wait for the Candle to CLOSE Below the Neckline
Not when it touches. Not when it wicks below. Wait for the close.
  • Candle closes below neckline → proceed to Step 6
  • Candle wicks below but closes above → do nothing, wait
6
Grade the Breakdown Candle Quality
Lower wick percentage:
<20%STRONG
Full 6
20–30%DECENT
Full 6
30–40%WEAK
4 or wait
>40%POOR
Skip/wait
7
Confirm with Stochastics 5,3,3
  • Stochastic already below 50 and falling, OR
  • Stochastic crossing down from above 80
  • BONUS: Bearish divergence (right top shows lower stochastic than left top)
If Stochastic is pegged below 20 and turning up → SKIP.
8
Enter Short with a Market Order
At the OPEN of the very next candle, hit market sell. 3–5 seconds.
9
Place Your Stop — 20 Ticks Default
Default stop = 20 ticks (2.0 pts M2K · 5.0 pts MES) above entry. Only tighten if structure (neckline, M top) is closer than 20 ticks away — never loosen past 20.
Example (M2K): Entry at 2299.0. Default stop at 2301.0 (20 ticks / 2.0 pts).
Risk = 2.0 pts × 24 contracts × $5/pt = $240.
Example (MES): Entry at 5800.00. Default stop at 5805.00 (20 ticks / 5.0 pts).
Risk = 5.0 pts × 8 contracts × $5/pt = $200.
10
Manage the Trade — 75/25 Auto-Scale
  • At +15 ticks (0.75R): ATM auto-scales 75% off (M2K: 18 of 24 · MES: 6 of 8) AND moves remaining stop to breakeven −1 tick.
  • At +30 ticks (1.5R): ATM auto-exits the 25% runner (M2K: 6 · MES: 2). Trade complete.
  • Hands off the mouse: The ATM template (M2K_075R_BE / MES_075R_BE) does all three exits automatically.
11
Time Stop
No movement within 3 candles (6 minutes) → EXIT.
🚩 Bull Flag · LONG
24 M2K / 8 MES · 20-tick stop · 75/25 split at 15 / 30 ticks
1
Identify the Pattern
  • Flagpole: 2–4 strong bullish candles (M2K: 15–30 pts / MES: 8–20 pts)
  • Flag: 4–12 candles of consolidation drifting slightly down or sideways
2
Validate the Flag — All Four Required
  • ☐ Retraces only 25–50% of flagpole (over 61.8% = NOT a bull flag, walk away)
  • ☐ Volume decreasing during the flag
  • ☐ Flag slopes slightly down or sideways (upward slope = ascending wedge = bearish)
  • ☐ Candles getting smaller (volatility compressing)
3
Draw the Flag Trendline
Across the highs of the pullback candles forming the flag. This is the upper boundary.
4
Watch and Wait
Order entry window ready. 6 contracts loaded. Finger near buy. Do NOT click yet.
5
Wait for the Candle to CLOSE Above the Flag Trendline
Not a wick. Not a poke. Wait for the close.
  • Candle closes above trendline → Step 6
  • Wick above but closes below → do nothing
6
Grade the Breakout Candle Quality
<20%STRONG
Full 6
20–30%DECENT
Full 6
30–40%WEAK
4 or wait
>40%POOR
Skip/retest
7
Confirm with Stochastics 5,3,3
  • Above 50 and rising, OR crossing up from below 20
  • Above 80 and rolling over → SKIP
8
Enter with a Market Order
At the OPEN of the very next candle, hit market buy. 3–5 seconds.
9
Place Your Stop — 20 Ticks Default
Default stop = 20 ticks (2.0 pts M2K · 5.0 pts MES) below entry. Only tighten if the broken flag trendline is closer than 20 ticks away — never loosen past 20, and NEVER drop down to the flag low (that's the old way).
Logic: The trendline was resistance; now support. If price closes back below, the breakout failed. No reason to ride it to the flag low.

Example (M2K): Entry at 2311.0. Default stop at 2309.0 (20 ticks / 2.0 pts).
Risk = 2.0 pts × 24 contracts × $5/pt = $240.

Example (MES): Entry at 5800.00. Default stop at 5795.00 (20 ticks / 5.0 pts).
Risk = 5.0 pts × 8 contracts × $5/pt = $200.
10
Manage the Trade — 75/25 Auto-Scale
  • At +15 ticks (0.75R): ATM auto-scales 75% off (M2K: 18 of 24 · MES: 6 of 8) AND moves remaining stop to breakeven +1 tick.
  • At +30 ticks (1.5R): ATM auto-exits the 25% runner (M2K: 6 · MES: 2). Trade complete.
  • Hands off the mouse: The ATM template (M2K_075R_BE / MES_075R_BE) does all three exits automatically.
11
Time Stop
No movement within 3 candles (6 minutes) → EXIT.
🏴 Bear Flag · SHORT
24 M2K / 8 MES · 20-tick stop · 75/25 split at 15 / 30 ticks · HTF filter mandatory · 2-candle time stop
1
Identify the Pattern
  • Flagpole: 2–4 strong bearish candles (M2K: 15–30 pt drop / MES: 8–20 pts)
  • Flag: 4–12 candles of consolidation drifting slightly up or sideways
2
Validate the Flag — All Four Required
  • ☐ Retraces only 25–50% of flagpole (over 61.8% = NOT a bear flag, walk away)
  • ☐ Volume decreasing during the flag
  • ☐ Flag slopes slightly up or sideways (downward slope = descending wedge = bullish)
  • ☐ Candles getting smaller
3
Higher Timeframe Filter — CRITICAL
Glance at 15-minute chart:
  • 15-min in clear uptrend → skip or max 2–3 contracts
  • 15-min sideways or down → normal size
More important than for bull flags. Most losing bear flags happen when shorting against higher-timeframe uptrend.
4
Draw the Flag Trendline
Across the lows of the bounce candles forming the flag. Lower boundary.
5
Watch and Wait
Order entry open. 6 contracts. Finger near sell.
6
Wait for the Candle to CLOSE Below the Flag Trendline
Wait for the close. Wicks don't count.
  • Candle closes below trendline → Step 7
  • Wick below but closes above → do nothing
7
Grade the Breakdown Candle Quality
<20%STRONG
Full pos.
20–30%DECENT
Full pos.
30–40%WEAK
4 or wait
>40%POOR
Skip/wait
8
Confirm with Stochastics 5,3,3
  • Below 50 and falling, OR crossing down from above 80
  • Below 20 and turning up → SKIP
9
Enter Short with a Market Order
At the OPEN of the very next candle, hit market sell.
10
Place Your Stop — 20 Ticks Default
Default stop = 20 ticks (2.0 pts M2K · 5.0 pts MES) above entry. Only tighten if the broken flag trendline is closer than 20 ticks away — never loosen past 20, and NEVER float up to the flag high (that's the old way).
Example (M2K): Entry at 2289.0. Default stop at 2291.0 (20 ticks / 2.0 pts).
Risk = 2.0 pts × 24 contracts × $5/pt = $240.
Example (MES): Entry at 5800.00. Default stop at 5805.00 (20 ticks / 5.0 pts).
Risk = 5.0 pts × 8 contracts × $5/pt = $200.
11
Manage the Trade — 75/25 Auto-Scale
Bear flags move faster and reverse more violently. The ATM handles all exits automatically.
  • At +15 ticks (0.75R): ATM auto-scales 75% off (M2K: 18 of 24 · MES: 6 of 8) AND moves remaining stop to breakeven −1 tick.
  • At +30 ticks (1.5R): ATM auto-exits the 25% runner (M2K: 6 · MES: 2). Trade complete.
  • Hands off the mouse: Template M2K_075R_BE / MES_075R_BE. You do not touch anything after entry.
12
Time Stop — TIGHTER THAN BULL FLAGS
No movement within 2 candles (4 minutes) → EXIT. Bear flags work fast or they don't work.
📋 The 10 Universal Rules
These apply to every trade, every pattern, every time.
  1. Always wait for the candle to CLOSE. No exceptions. Not with 10 seconds left. Not when it "looks impossible to fail."
  2. Always grade the breakout candle wick. Under 20% = strong. 20–30% = decent. 30–40% = weak. Over 40% = skip.
  3. Always use the 20-tick default stop. 20 ticks = 2.0 pts M2K / 5.0 pts MES. Tighten ONLY if structure (neckline, broken trendline) is closer than 20 ticks away. Never loosen past 20. Never at the W bottom, M top, flag low, or flag high.
  4. Always confirm with Stochastics 5,3,3. Momentum must align with trade direction.
  5. Always check the 15-minute chart for shorts. Double tops and bear flags require the higher timeframe to NOT be in clear uptrend.
  6. Auto-breakeven at +15 ticks (0.75R). The ATM moves the runner stop to breakeven the instant T1 fills. You do not touch the mouse.
  7. Take 75% off at +15 ticks (T1 / 0.75R). M2K: 18 of 24 contracts. MES: 6 of 8 contracts. ATM fires it automatically.
  8. Always use a time stop. 3 candles (6 min) for double bottoms/tops/bull flags. 2 candles (4 min) for bear flags.
  9. Always enter with a market order at the open of the next candle. No buy stops. No limit orders.
  10. Always screenshot every trade — wins AND losses. Review weekly.
💡 Quick Reference Card
Pattern Entry Stop Time Stop HTF Filter
Double Bottom Market buy after close above neckline 20 ticks BELOW entry (tighten if neckline closer) 3 candles Optional
Double Top Market sell after close below neckline 20 ticks ABOVE entry (tighten if neckline closer) 3 candles Mandatory
Bull Flag Market buy after close above trendline 20 ticks BELOW entry (tighten if trendline closer) 3 candles Optional
Bear Flag Market sell after close below trendline 20 ticks ABOVE entry (tighten if trendline closer) 2 candles Mandatory
All patterns: M2K 24 (18/6 split) · MES 8 (6/2 split) · 20-tick default stop · T1 = +15 ticks (75% off) · T2 = +30 ticks (25% runner) · Auto-breakeven at T1 · ATM templates M2K_075R_BE / MES_075R_BE.
⚠️ The Two Mindset Traps
Trap 1 · The "Runaway" Fear
When you see a big green candle explode above the neckline with 15 seconds left, your brain screams "it'll run away without me forever!" 85–90% of the time the next candle pulls back and gives you a fair entry. The 10–15% that truly run away are often the most dangerous to chase — parabolic moves exhaust themselves quickly.
Missing a trade costs ZERO dollars. Getting trapped costs real money.
Trap 2 · The Buy Stop Comfort
Buy stops feel calm because they remove the decision. But they trigger on wicks, which is incompatible with confirmation entries. Comfort and profitability are not the same thing. The decision IS the edge. You are now a confirmation trader.

🆕 v5 Appendix · Candle Patterns · Stacking · Calculator · Kill Switches

The v5 expansion adds three single/multi-candle reversal patterns (Hammer·Shooting Star, Engulfing, Morning·Evening Star), a formal pattern-stacking framework, a standalone position calculator, and one new daily kill switch. Everything below integrates into the same 2-minute workflow — no changes to the DB/DT/BF/BEF cards above.

📋 The Daily Hunt List (v5)

Before 9:30 ET you write a hunt list: 2–5 named setups you'll actively look for today. If none fire, you don't trade — that's discipline, not missed opportunity. v5 adds three new hunt categories:

🔨 Hammer / Shooting Star — at named levels · midday + afternoon Best used when the market is flat-ish or range-bound. Requires: prior 3+ candle directional move INTO a named level (PDH, PDL, ONH, ONL, VWAP, whole number), wick ≥2× body, body within 20% of the opposite extreme, volume ≥1.2× 20-period avg. Kill switch if no volume confirmation.
🫂 Bullish / Bearish Engulfing — at named levels · all session blocks The most versatile candle pattern. Requires: body completely engulfs prior candle's body (wicks don't count), close in top/bottom 25% of candle range, ≥1.2× avg volume, clear prior trend of 3+ candles. Best when it fires AT the neckline break of a DB/DT. That's where engulfing earns its keep.
⭐ Morning / Evening Star — after extended moves · any block The rarest but highest-probability pattern. Requires: 3 specific candles (strong directional + small indecision + strong reversal), middle candle gaps or holds, reversal candle recaptures ≥50% of first candle's range. Best after 5+ candle trends or at exhaustion volume spikes.

🛑 Updated Stop Rule Summary

Every pattern in v5 now supports two stop variants. Classic is safer; Tight is smaller dollar risk per contract, enabling bigger size on stacked setups within the daily risk cap.

📐 Point-based buffers — v5 update All stops are measured in points. Per-instrument buffer from the chosen stop anchor:
  • MES — 1.0 pt
  • MNQ — 1.5 pt
  • M2K — 0.4 pt
  • ES — 1.0 pt
  • NQ — 2.0 pt
  • RTY — 0.4 pt
Each pattern's section 4b shows the exact point calculation for both Classic and Tight variants.
🔑 Which variant when?
  • Classic: single-pattern trades, new to a setup, morning trades, higher volatility
  • Tight: stacked A+/A++ setups, patterns you've logged 20+ wins on, midday trades, after a winning streak gives you confidence in the level

🔗 Pattern Stacking Workflow

Stacking is what turns A-grade trades into A+ and A++ trades. The workflow is deliberate and repeatable:

Step 1 · Pre-market mark-up (before 9:30 ET) Mark on every chart: PDH, PDL, ONH, ONL, daily VWAP anchor, round numbers, prior week highs/lows. Write 2–3 hunt entries: "Hunt hammer long at MES PDL near 4317" etc. These become your stacking candidates — stacks only form at marked levels.
Step 2 · Session trigger watch When price approaches a marked level, start watching for a multi-candle pattern (DB, DT, bull flag, bear flag). When that pattern starts forming, start watching for a single-candle trigger (hammer, star, engulfing) at its completion. Each tier adds a layer.
Step 3 · Score the stack (0–5) Run the 5-criterion quality check from the Pattern Stacking tab before sizing: named / same direction / same price / within 4 candles / HTF agrees. Score ≥4 = size up. Score <4 = treat as single pattern, standard size.
Step 4 · Size per hierarchy Single pattern = 4 contracts (standard). 2-pattern stack = 6 contracts (+50%). 3-pattern A++ stack = 8 contracts (+100%). Always run the Position Calculator to confirm dollar risk is inside the $600 daily cap — never break that, even on A++.
Step 5 · Log the stack type every trade Journal field: "Stack — none / 2-pattern / 3-pattern" + stack score 0–5 + pattern names ("hammer + DB at PDL"). Weekly review: compare win rate / avg R across stack counts. If 3-stack isn't outperforming 1-stack, you're being too generous with what counts as a stack — recalibrate.

🧮 Calculator Workflow

The Position Calculator v2 is the enforcement layer. It's not optional — every trade, before clicking, it tells you exactly how many contracts you can take for $200 risk given the stop on the chart.

⚡ Reverse mode — use every trade, before entry
  1. Enter target risk ($200 default · $100 after a loss · $50 after two losses)
  2. Enter stop distance from the chart (measure pts from entry to your chosen Classic or Tight stop)
  3. Pick the instrument (MES / MNQ / M2K / RTY / ES / NQ)
  4. Calculator returns contract count — use that exact number, no rounding up
📐 Fibonacci mode — use for TP ladders Enter the pattern low and high (e.g., DB's deeper low and neckline peak). Calculator returns 0.5 / 0.618 / 1.272 / 1.618 extensions as T1/T2/T3/T4 targets with R multiples. Handy when price is moving fast and you don't want to math it in your head.
📊 EV mode — use during weekly review Expectancy = (win% × avg win R) − (loss% × avg loss R). Plug in last week's journal numbers. If +EV, keep trading. If flat or −EV, reduce size and investigate — most likely culprits: grading stacks too generously, skipping quality score, or trading outside the hunt list.

🛑 Updated Daily Kill Switches

Existing kill switches remain (2-loss lockout, max 3 trades, 11:30–13:30 lunch lock). v5 adds one:

🆕 v5 Kill Switch — Stack Failure Protection Rule: Max 2 losing stacked trades in a row → stop trading A+ setups for the rest of the session. You can still take A-grade (single-pattern) setups, but at reduced size (half standard) and only from your original hunt list.

Why: Two losing stacks in a row is rare and signals either (a) your stack-grading is miscalibrated today or (b) market conditions invalidate the stacking edge (news, low-liquidity, structural shift). Either way, the expensive mistake is taking a third sized-up trade. Step down until tomorrow.
🛑 Full v5 Kill-Switch List — memorize
  • 2 losses on the day → lock out for the session (full lockout)
  • 3 trades taken (win or lose) → no more trades today
  • 11:30–13:30 ET → no trades (lunch lock) unless A++ stack at named level
  • 2 losing stacks in a row → no more A+ trades today · A-grade only at half size
  • News event within next 15 min → flatten all positions · no new entries until 5 min after release
  • State > Calm on the State Gate → no new entries until re-gate to Calm
  • Non-hunt-list trade — if the setup isn't on today's hunt list, it's automatically disqualified
This is the plan. Follow it. Trust the process. Trust the research.
A. Shapiro, MD · Arnie's Day Trading Setup Playbook · MES · M2K · MNQ · RTY · v5.0 Expanded
"Context + Trigger + Risk. Never two out of three."
LEARN · TRADE · REVIEW — the complete 2-minute scalping workflow in one app
